surviving.title: Upgrade react-router-dom past turbo-stream RCE / DoS advisories
surviving.description: The lockfile resolves react-router and react-router-dom to 7.14.0. This version is in the vulnerable range for GHSA-49rj-9fvp-4h2h (vendored turbo-stream v2 allows arbitrary constructor invocation via TYPE_ERROR deserialization -> RCE, fixed in 7.14.2), GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78 (DoS via unbounded path expansion in the __manifest endpoint, fixed in 7.15.0), and GHSA-2j2x-hqr9-3h42 (open redirect via protocol-relative // paths, fixed in 7.14.1). The RCE/manifest-DoS surface primarily affects React Router framework/SSR mode; this app uses createBrowserRouter as a pure SPA on GitHub Pages with no server runtime, which substantially reduces real exploitability. But the package.json range '^7.14.0' deliberately floors at the vulnerable build and the open-redirect class is reachable client-side.
surviving.evidence: package.json: "react-router-dom": "^7.14.0"; pnpm-lock.yaml: react-router-dom@7.14.0 / react-router@7.14.0; pnpm audit reports 6 high + 6 moderate + 1 critical including 'React Router's vendored turbo-stream v2 ... leading to Unauth RCE' (Vulnerable >=7.0.0 <=7.14.1, Patched >=7.14.2).
surviving.recommendation: Run `pnpm up react-router-dom@latest` (or pin to >=7.15.0) and regenerate pnpm-lock.yaml; react-router-dom 7.x has zero breaking changes from 7.14 to 7.15. Then confirm with `pnpm audit --prod` that the three react-router rows (GHSA-49rj-9fvp-4h2h, GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78, GHSA-2j2x-hqr9-3h42) are gone. Note: the headline RCE and __manifest DoS only apply to React Router framework/SSR mode; this app is a pure createBrowserRouter SPA on GitHub Pages with no server runtime, no loaders/actions, and no redirect sink that consumes attacker-controlled URL input, so real-world exploitability is low — treat as routine dependency-hygiene patching rather than an urgent fix. The larger live audit signal is actually protobufjs (1 critical + several high, via firebase>@firebase/firestore>@grpc/proto-loader), which warrants equal or higher attention.
surviving.verifier_note:
Core facts verified. package.json:35 = "react-router-dom": "^7.14.0". pnpm-lock.yaml pins react-router-dom@7.14.0 and react-router@7.14.0 (lines 1570/1577 declarations, 3485/3491 resolutions). `pnpm audit --prod` confirms all three react-router advisories with ranges matching the finding exactly: GHSA-49rj-9fvp-4h2h (turbo-stream RCE) vulnerable >=7.0.0 <=7.14.1, patched >=7.14.2; GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78 (manifest DoS) vulnerable >=7.0.0 <7.15.0, patched >=7.15.0; GHSA-2j2x-hqr9-3h42 (open redirect) vulnerable >=7.0.0 <7.14.1, patched >=7.14.1. Installed 7.14.0 is in all three vulnerable ranges. Proposed fix (bump to >=7.15.0) is correct — it is the highest patch floor and clears all three rows.

Where the finding is mis-scoped: severity HIGH overstates real exploitability for THIS app. The two serious classes (RCE via turbo-stream deserialization, __manifest endpoint DoS) require React Router framework/SSR mode. This app is a pure client SPA: src/router.tsx:21 uses createBrowserRouter, src/main.tsx:11 uses RouterProvider, there is no entry.server, no loaders/actions, and no __manifest server endpoint (the functions/ dir is unrelated Firebase Functions). The finding honestly discloses this caveat, but then keeps HIGH severity. The remaining claim — "the open-redirect class is reachable client-side" — is the weakest: I grepped all redirect sinks (src/components/*Redirect.tsx, RedirectToExternal.tsx, PrivateRoute.tsx) and every window.location.href / Navigate target is a hardcoded constant or internal location object; none consume attacker-controlled URL params, so there is no demonstrated open-redirect sink in the app's own code. Given a trivial fix but no exploitable path in this SPA, this is dependency hygiene best rated medium, not high. Facts, evidence, and fix are otherwise sound.

surviving.title: firebase pulls protobufjs <7.5.8 (critical RCE + several high DoS advisories)
surviving.description: `pnpm audit --prod` flags a critical 'Arbitrary code execution in protobufjs' (<7.5.5, GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg) plus multiple high/moderate code-injection, prototype-pollution and DoS advisories in protobufjs, transitively dragged in by firebase's Firestore gRPC loader. In the browser bundle Firestore uses the WebChannel/REST transport rather than the gRPC proto path, so the practical exploitability in this SPA is limited; however the dependency is present in the production dependency tree and is reported as critical, so it should not be left unremediated.
surviving.evidence: pnpm audit --prod: 'critical | Arbitrary code execution in protobufjs | Vulnerable versions <7.5.5 | Paths .>firebase>@firebase/firestore>@grpc/proto-loader>protobufjs'. Summary: 13 vulnerabilities found Severity: 6 moderate | 6 high | 1 critical.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read package.json and ran `pnpm audit --prod` plus `pnpm why protobufjs` against the actual lockfile. The finding is factually accurate on every checkable point:

1. package.json declares `"firebase": "^12.1.0"` (it is line 28, not 29 as cited — minor cite error). It resolves to firebase@12.12.0.
2. `pnpm why protobufjs` confirms exactly one version, protobufjs@7.5.4 (pnpm-lock.yaml line 1546), via the exact path firebase > @firebase/firestore@4.14.0 > @grpc/proto-loader@0.7.15 > protobufjs.
3. 7.5.4 is `<7.5.5`, so the critical "Arbitrary code execution in protobufjs" (GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg, patched >=7.5.5) applies, plus the multiple high/moderate code-injection, prototype-pollution and DoS advisories (patched >=7.5.6).
4. The audit summary matches the claimed evidence verbatim: "13 vulnerabilities found / Severity: 6 moderate | 6 high | 1 critical." (Note: 6 of the highs/moderates and the critical are protobufjs; the remaining high/moderate entries are react-router advisories on the same path, a separate finding.)
5. The browser-exploitability caveat is correct: this is a web SPA importing the Firebase web SDK, whose Firestore uses WebChannel/REST in browsers; @grpc/proto-loader/protobufjs is the Node gRPC code path and is not exercised in the shipped browser bundle. So real-world exploitability of the deployed site is low.

Why partial rather than confirmed: the proposed fix is partly wrong. "Update firebase to the latest 12.x (which pulls a fixed @grpc/proto-loader/protobufjs)" is unreliable — `npm view @grpc/proto-loader` shows even the latest 0.8.1 still declares `protobufjs: "^7.5.5"`, and 7.5.5 is still flagged by the high/moderate advisories (patched >=7.5.6). A firebase bump alone may therefore leave the highs unresolved. The pnpm `overrides` route IS the dependable fix, and the >=7.5.8 floor is valid (7.5.8 and 7.5.9 are published and clear all listed advisories).

The "high" severity is defensible at the finding level (a critical advisory is present in the prod tree, but practical browser exploitability is low), so I leave severity unchanged and only correct the fix.

surviving.title: Cloud Function dependency tree has 2 critical + 2 high advisories (firebase-admin/google-gax chain)
surviving.description: `npm audit` inside functions/ reports 17 vulnerabilities (1 low, 12 moderate, 2 high, 2 critical) coming from the firebase-admin -> google-gax -> gaxios/teeny-request/retry-request/uuid chain. These ship in the deployed translateText Cloud Function runtime. Even though most are DoS/SSRF-adjacent in transitive google-cloud libraries the function does not directly exercise, the deployed runtime should be patched. Note functions/package.json pins firebase-admin ^12.0.0 while the TRMNL workflow separately pins firebase-admin ^14 — the deployed function is on the older major.
surviving.evidence: functions npm audit tail: '17 vulnerabilities (1 low, 12 moderate, 2 high, 2 critical)'; chain shows gaxios 6.4.0-6.7.1, google-gax, teeny-request, retry-request depending on vulnerable uuid.
surviving.recommendation: In functions/, run `npm audit fix --force`, which upgrades firebase-admin to 14.0.0 (a breaking major) and pulls in patched protobufjs/fast-xml-parser/node-forge/path-to-regexp/uuid via the refreshed @google-cloud/firestore + express trees. A plain `npm audit fix` and any 12.x/13.x patch bump resolve nothing (verified by dry-run). After upgrading, smoke-test the translateText function (admin.initializeApp / admin.auth().verifyIdToken in functions/src/index.ts must still work under firebase-admin v14's modular API), then redeploy. Doing so also aligns functions/ with the TRMNL workflow's firebase-admin@^14 pin, eliminating the divergent-major problem. Note the genuinely high-priority advisories to confirm are resolved are protobufjs (arbitrary code execution) and node-forge (signature forgery / cert-chain bypass) — not the gaxios/uuid chain named in the original finding.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read functions/package.json (line 16 pins firebase-admin "^12.0.0", confirmed) and ran npm audit in functions/. The headline counts are accurate verbatim: "17 vulnerabilities (1 low, 12 moderate, 2 high, 2 critical)". These deps DO ship in the deployed translateText function — functions/src/index.ts:2,6,57 imports firebase-admin and calls admin.auth().verifyIdToken. The firebase-admin major divergence (functions on ^12 vs TRMNL workflow on ^14) is also real. So the existence/severity of the issue is confirmed.

Two material parts are wrong, hence partial:

1) MISATTRIBUTED ADVISORIES. The finding claims the 2 critical + 2 high come from the "firebase-admin -> google-gax -> gaxios/teeny-request/retry-request/uuid chain." Parsing `npm audit --json` shows the actual critical/high advisories are: protobufjs <=7.5.7 (CRITICAL — arbitrary code execution GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg, plus multiple HIGH), fast-xml-parser <=5.6.0 (CRITICAL — regex injection GHSA-m7jm-9gc2-mpf2, plus HIGH), node-forge <=1.3.3 (HIGH — cert-chain bypass / signature forgery), and path-to-regexp <0.1.13 (HIGH — ReDoS). By contrast gaxios (6.4.0-6.7.1), google-gax (4.0.5-4.6.1), teeny-request, retry-request and uuid (<11.1.1) are all only MODERATE/LOW. So the cited chain is NOT the source of the critical/high; the title is a misattribution. protobufjs (arbitrary code exec) and node-forge (signature forgery / cert bypass) are the genuinely serious ones, and these aren't even mentioned.

2) PROPOSED FIX IS NON-FUNCTIONAL. firebase-admin "^12" is already resolved to 12.7.0 (the latest 12.x — verified via npm view), so a 12.x patch bump changes nothing. The audit flags the entire firebase-admin range 10.2.0-13.10.0 as vulnerable, so bumping to 13.x (latest 13.10.0) also does NOT clear it. `npm audit fix` WITHOUT --force resolves 0 of 17 (verified: dry-run still reports "17 vulnerabilities (1 low, 12 moderate, 2 high, 2 critical)"). Only `npm audit fix --force` fixes them, and it does so by installing firebase-admin@14.0.0 — explicitly flagged "which is a breaking change." So the proposed "bump to 12.x/13.x patch + npm audit fix" cannot work.

Severity stays medium: the function is auth-gated (Bearer token + verifyIdToken + 3-email allowlist + rate limit) and most CVEs are DoS/SSRF-adjacent in google-cloud transitive libs the function doesn't exercise via untrusted input; protobufjs RCE requires attacker-controlled proto definitions, not present here. So medium is fair — not higher.

surviving.title: translateText accepts unbounded/untyped `text`, enabling cost-amplification abuse
surviving.description: The function only checks `if (!text)` before forwarding `req.body.text` straight to the Google Translate paid API. There is no maximum-length check, no string-type check, and no array-size limit. An authenticated whitelisted user (or anyone who phishes/steals one of the three Google sessions) can POST a multi-megabyte `text` string, or `text` as a giant array, billing the project's Translate API at attacker-chosen volume. text.length and text.substring are also called assuming a string; sending `text` as a number/object would throw and surface as a 500. Combined with the per-instance rate limiter below, the real cap on spend is weak.
surviving.evidence: functions/src/index.ts:94 `const { text, sourceLanguage } = req.body;` :95 `if (!text) { ... }` then :101 `text.length` / :119 `q: text` forwarded directly to translation.googleapis.com.
surviving.recommendation: Add strict input validation right after destructuring at line 94, before the textLength log at line 101: `if (typeof text !== 'string' || text.length === 0 || text.length > 5000) { res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid text parameter' }); return; }`. This single check fixes the missing type guard (preventing the TypeError-to-500 path at line 101) and the missing length bound (the genuine per-character billing-amplification vector) in one shot. Additionally validate sourceLanguage against an allow-list as defense-in-depth: `const allowedSources = ['da', 'hi']; const source = allowedSources.includes(sourceLanguage) ? sourceLanguage : 'da';` then pass `source` at line 120 instead of `sourceLanguage || 'da'`. Note: an explicit array-handling branch is NOT required, since arrays already fail the `typeof text !== 'string'` guard — contrary to the original finding, arrays never reached the API.
surviving.verifier_note:
I re-read functions/src/index.ts in full. The core claims are accurate. Line 94 is `const { text, sourceLanguage } = req.body;` and the ONLY validation is line 95 `if (!text) {`. Line 101 calls `text.length` and `text.substring(0, 50)` assuming a string, and lines 118-120 forward `q: text` / `source: sourceLanguage || 'da'` directly to translation.googleapis.com (the paid API). So: no max-length check (confirmed), no string-type check (confirmed), and no allow-list on sourceLanguage (confirmed). The type-confusion-to-500 claim is correct: a numeric `text` passes `!text`, then `text.substring` throws TypeError, caught at line 152 and surfaced as a generic 500.

However, one sub-claim is WRONG: the "send text as a giant array to amplify billing" vector does NOT work in this code. If `text` is an array, line 101's `text.substring(0, 50)` throws TypeError (arrays have no .substring) and is caught at line 152, returning 500 BEFORE the fetch at line 113 ever runs. So an array never reaches the Translate API. The real, valid cost-amplification vector is a single multi-megabyte STRING (Google Translate v2 bills per character), which passes all checks and is forwarded verbatim.

Severity should be downgraded from medium to low. The endpoint is NOT open to the internet: it requires a valid Firebase ID token (lines 49-57) AND membership in a 3-email allow-list (lines 60-64), plus a 30-req/min per-uid rate limiter (lines 66-79). The threat reduces to "a trusted whitelisted user, or someone who has compromised one of 3 specific Google accounts, sends oversized strings." That is a real input-validation/hardening gap and the fix is cheap, but the trusted-user gate caps practical exposure far below a medium finding. The mis-stated array vector and the overstated "anyone who phishes a session" framing (still requires compromising one of 3 trusted accounts) justify partial rather than confirmed.

surviving.title: In-memory rate limiter is ineffective on Cloud Functions (per-instance, resets on cold start)
surviving.description: The rate limiter is a module-level `Map<uid, timestamp[]>`. Cloud Functions (gen1 onRequest) scale horizontally and recycle instances, so each concurrent instance keeps its own Map and a cold start wipes it. An attacker can exceed 30 req/min by simply driving concurrency (each new instance grants a fresh 30-request budget) and the limit silently provides far less protection than it appears to. Given that the only authorized callers are 3 trusted users this is low severity, but it is the sole brake on Translate API spend and should not be relied upon as written.
surviving.evidence: functions/src/index.ts:18 `const rateLimiter = new Map<string, number[]>();` used at :69-79; deployed as `functions.https.onRequest` (gen1) with no maxInstances/concurrency config in firebase.json.
surviving.recommendation: Enforce the rate limit in a shared store (Firestore counter doc keyed by uid+minute, or Firebase App Check + a quota), and cap function cost with `maxInstances`. At minimum document that the in-memory limiter is best-effort.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read functions/src/index.ts and firebase.json directly. Every claim checks out:

(1) index.ts:18 `const rateLimiter = new Map<string, number[]>();` is genuinely module-level (declared at top scope, outside the handler), so it is per-instance process state.

(2) It is used at index.ts:69 (`rateLimiter.get(uid)`), :70 (filter to RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW=60000ms), :72 (compare against RATE_LIMIT_MAX=30), :78-79 (push now + `rateLimiter.set`). The cleanup at :82-91 only runs when `rateLimiter.size > 100` and operates on that same per-instance Map, so it does nothing to share state across instances.

(3) Deployment is gen1: `functions.https.onRequest` (index.ts:22) via the v1 default namespace import `import * as functions from 'firebase-functions'` (index.ts:1). firebase-functions is ^7.0.2 but this is the legacy v1 onRequest, which is gen1 unless explicitly migrated to firebase-functions/v2 (it is not).

(4) No instance/concurrency limit exists anywhere: firebase.json contains only `{ functions: { source: "functions" }, firestore: {...} }`, and a repo-wide grep for maxInstances|setGlobalOptions|concurrency|runWith returns zero non-lockfile matches. So there is no maxInstances cap on cost either.

The core technical claim is therefore correct: in-memory module state on horizontally-scaling, cold-start-recycling gen1 functions provides only best-effort, per-instance rate limiting. An attacker driving concurrency obtains a fresh 30-req/min budget per instance, and (per code) this is the only programmatic brake on Google Translate API spend.

Severity 'low' is appropriate and the finding itself justifies it: the endpoint is gated by Firebase Auth token verification (index.ts:50-57) plus a 3-email allowlist (index.ts:60), so reaching the rate limiter at all requires a valid token from one of 3 trusted accounts. The limiter is defense-in-depth / cost control, not a primary security boundary — which keeps real-world exploitability low. The proposed fix (shared Firestore counter or App Check + quota, plus maxInstances cap, or at minimum documenting it as best-effort) is accurate and proportionate.

surviving.title: LovesIngy silently parses message input as JSON and writes attacker-controlled message/timestamp
surviving.description: handleAddLoveMessage tries `JSON.parse(newLoveMessage)`; if it parses to an object it overwrites the stored document with `message: parsedMessage.text` and `timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp/1000, parsedMessage.timestamp%1000)`. A user typing a JSON object can (a) set `message` to undefined (if `.text` is absent) which then breaks the TRMNL sync's `.startsWith('>>>')`/`.replace` calls and the UI's stripTrmnlPrefix, and (b) forge an arbitrary historical timestamp, reordering/poisoning the feed and the TRMNL 'throwback' selection. This is restricted to the 3 whitelisted users so it's a data-integrity foot-gun rather than an external exploit, but it is undocumented implicit behavior on user input. NOTE: rendering itself is safe — React escapes {message} as text, so there is no XSS here or in VisitsDenmark/GivesIngy/FeedbackForm.
surviving.evidence: src/components/LovesIngy/LovesIngy.tsx:163 `const parsedMessage = JSON.parse(newLoveMessage);` :166 `message: parsedMessage.text,` :167 `timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp / 1000, parsedMessage.timestamp % 1000),`
surviving.recommendation: Remove the implicit JSON-parsing branch (it appears to be a seeding/import hack), or guard it behind a validated shape: require both `parsedMessage.text` is a non-empty string and `parsedMessage.timestamp` is a finite number before using them; otherwise store the raw string. Add a Firestore rules constraint that `message is string` and length-limited.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read src/components/LovesIngy/LovesIngy.tsx:147-189 and the claim holds exactly. handleAddLoveMessage builds messageData with the raw string + serverTimestamp() (156-159), then at 162-172 does `JSON.parse(newLoveMessage)` and, if it parses to an object (164), OVERWRITES with `message: parsedMessage.text` (166) and `timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp / 1000, parsedMessage.timestamp % 1000)` (167). There is no validation that `.text` is a non-empty string or that `.timestamp` is a finite number, then it `addDoc`s the result (174). So a whitelisted user typing e.g. `{"foo":1}` writes `message: undefined` and an Invalid-Date Timestamp.

Downstream impact (a) is real and slightly understated. UI render at 373 calls `stripTrmnlPrefix(message)` -> `msg.replace(/^>>>\s*/,'')` with NO guard, so an undefined message throws during render. In scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs the filter at :53 uses optional chaining (`doc.data().message?.startsWith('>>>')`) so it tolerates undefined, BUT the selection path at :75-78 does `selectedDoc.data().message.replace(...)` with NO optional chaining — if the odd-hour deterministic-random selector (:59-63 picks any doc from the last 200 by seed%length) lands on a doc whose message is undefined, the cron sync throws TypeError and fails. So the finding's reference to `.replace` breaking is accurate.

Downstream impact (b) is real: both UI (:57) and sync (:35) `orderBy('timestamp','desc')`, and the odd-hour throwback branch (:59-68) reads/reports `selectedDoc.data().timestamp` for `throwbackDate`. A forged timestamp therefore reorders the feed and can poison/relabel the throwback. (Minor: a non-numeric timestamp yields NaN -> Invalid Timestamp rather than a clean forged date, but a numeric value forges arbitrary dates as claimed.)

XSS note is correct: LovesIngy.tsx:373 and :366 render `{message}` as escaped React text children (no dangerouslySetInnerHTML), so no XSS. firestore.rules:5-12 confirms `love-ingy-messages` is restricted to 3 whitelisted emails with no `message is string`/length constraint, so the rules-hardening half of the fix addresses a genuine gap. Severity "low" is correct: exploitation requires one of 3 trusted users to deliberately enter a JSON object, and the harm is self-inflicted data corruption / a crashed cron job, not an external exploit. The proposed fix (validate shape before using `.text`/`.timestamp`, else store raw string; add Firestore `message is string` + length rule) is sound.

surviving.title: trmnl-sync.mjs JSON.parse on countdown event fields trusted from client-writable doc
surviving.description: The scheduled sync reads the trmnl-config/countdowns document and does `new Date(event.date + 'T00:00:00')` and uses event.name/event.emoji directly. That document is writable by all three whitelisted users via LovesIngy.saveCountdowns with no server-side schema validation (Firestore rules only check the caller's email, not field shapes). A malformed `event.date` yields NaN diffDays (filtered out) and a non-string name flows into the TRMNL payload. Impact is limited to breaking the user's own e-ink display, but the sync has no defensive validation of fields it does not control.
surviving.evidence: scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs:101 `const eventDate = new Date(event.date + 'T00:00:00');` :105-108 `name: event.name` forwarded to the TRMNL webhook payload; firestore.rules trmnl-config block validates only `request.auth.token.email`, not document fields.
surviving.recommendation: Correct the title to remove the nonexistent "JSON.parse on countdown event fields" claim — the issue is missing input validation on already-deserialized Firestore object fields, not a parse sink. Then, as defense-in-depth: in trmnl-sync.mjs, validate each event before mapping — skip entries where typeof event.name !== 'string', cap name length (e.g. event.name.slice(0,80)), require event.date to match /^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/, and verify !isNaN(eventDate.getTime()). Optionally add field-shape constraints to the firestore.rules trmnl-config write rule (e.g. request.resource.data.events is a list, each with string name/date). Given the only writers are 3 trusted whitelisted users and the sole failure mode is a garbled self-owned display, this is hardening, not an urgent fix.
surviving.verifier_note:
I re-read scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs, firestore.rules, and src/components/LovesIngy/LovesIngy.tsx.

TITLE IS FACTUALLY WRONG: The title claims "JSON.parse on countdown event fields." There is NO JSON.parse on countdown event fields anywhere. Lines 99-113 operate on config.events, which is an already-deserialized Firestore object array (config = configDoc.data() at line 94). The code accesses event.date/event.name/event.emoji as plain object properties. The ONLY JSON.parse in the file is line 18 (process.env.FIREBASE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT) — a trusted CI secret, entirely unrelated to countdowns. A fixer reading the title would look for a parse sink that doesn't exist.

BODY/EVIDENCE IS MOSTLY ACCURATE: trmnl-sync.mjs:101 `const eventDate = new Date(event.date + 'T00:00:00')` exists verbatim. Lines 104-109 forward `name: event.name` (no fallback/coercion) and `emoji: event.emoji || '📅'` into the payload. firestore.rules lines 15-22 (trmnl-config block) gate writes only on request.auth.token.email — no field-shape validation. LovesIngy.tsx:106 does `setDoc(docRef, { events: updatedEvents })` with zero server-side schema validation. So the "client-writable doc with no field validation" half of the claim is correct.

IMPACT IS CORRECTLY SCOPED AS MINIMAL, AND THE BODY ADMITS IT: The only writers are 3 hardcoded whitelisted emails (trusted family members), not arbitrary clients. A malformed event.date produces NaN diffDays which is filtered out by line 111 (`e.days > 0`), so it cannot crash the loop. A non-string event.name flows into the JSON payload, but the payload is POSTed as JSON to a webhook (line 153) — there is no HTML/SQL/shell injection sink, no privilege boundary crossed, no cross-user impact. Worst case: a fully-trusted user garbles their own e-ink display. This is defense-in-depth hardening, not a vulnerability. Severity "low" is, if anything, slightly generous (info would be defensible), so I leave severity unchanged at low rather than escalating.

Net: the underlying observation (no input validation on a client-writable doc consumed by the sync) is real and the proposed fix is reasonable, but the headline mischaracterizes the mechanism (invents a JSON.parse sink), so this is partial, not confirmed.

surviving.title: /private route is not wrapped in PrivateRoute (auth-gate inconsistency)
surviving.description: The /private route renders <PrivateApp/> with only an ErrorBoundary and no <PrivateRoute> wrapper, unlike /lovesingy, /progress, /translate. PrivateApp is currently just the default Vite+React demo page (logo + counter + logout button) and touches no Firestore data, so there is no data exposure today. Flagging it as a latent access-control inconsistency: if this route is ever repurposed to show private content it will be publicly reachable. The real authorization boundary remains Firestore rules, which are correctly enforced regardless of client routing.
surviving.evidence: src/router.tsx:39 `path: '/private'` element renders `<ErrorBoundary><PrivateApp /></ErrorBoundary>` with no PrivateRoute, vs :54-63 /lovesingy which uses `<PrivateRoute>`.
surviving.recommendation: Wrap /private in <PrivateRoute> for consistency, or delete the route and the demo PrivateApp component if unused.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read all three relevant files. src/router.tsx:38-45 renders the `/private` route as `<ErrorBoundary><PrivateApp /></ErrorBoundary>` with no `<PrivateRoute>` wrapper, exactly as claimed. By contrast /lovesingy (lines 54-63), /progress (64-73), and /translate (74-83) all wrap their elements in `<PrivateRoute>`. So the inconsistency is real and verbatim accurate.

src/components/PrivateApp/PrivateApp.tsx is indeed the stock Vite+React demo: it has a `count` useState counter, Vite/React logo links, and a Logout button. It imports only `auth` (for `signOut`) and does NOT import `db` or touch Firestore at all. Therefore there is genuinely no private data exposure on this route today — the finding correctly states this.

src/components/PrivateRoute.tsx confirms the auth gate is purely client-side (`user ? children : <Navigate to="/login">`), which is UX-level only and trivially bypassable, so the finding's claim that the true authorization boundary is Firestore rules (not client routing) is correct.

Severity `info` is appropriate: this is a latent access-control consistency issue with zero current exposure, not an exploitable vulnerability. The finding does not overstate risk, correctly notes the absence of data exposure, and offers a sound fix (wrap in PrivateRoute for consistency, or delete the unused demo route/component). All cited line ranges and component behaviors match the actual code.

surviving.title: Verbose error and PII logging in translateText Cloud Function
surviving.description: The function logs request origin, the authenticated user's email and uid, a 50-char preview of every translation input and output, and full error stacks to Cloud Logging. The translated content is the user's private speech (Danish/Hindi conversations). This persists PII and message content in GCP logs. Low risk given a single-project owner, but worth noting as data-minimization hygiene; it also means raised exceptions return only a generic 'Translation failed' to clients (good) while the detailed stack stays server-side (fine).
surviving.evidence: functions/src/index.ts:58 `console.log('Auth token verified', { email: decodedToken.email, uid: decodedToken.uid });` :101 `preview: text.substring(0, 50)` :147 `preview: translatedText.substring(0, 50)`.
surviving.recommendation: Drop or debug-gate the input/output previews (lines 101 and 147) and the email field at line 58 — log uid alone for correlation rather than email+uid alongside content. Note the email is also logged at lines 61 and 73; gate or remove those too if minimizing PII. Set a short retention policy on these function logs in Cloud Logging.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read functions/src/index.ts in full. Every cited line check out exactly as the finding claims:

- Line 24: `console.log('Translation request received', { method: req.method, origin: req.headers.origin });` — logs request origin. Confirmed.
- Line 58: `console.log('Auth token verified', { email: decodedToken.email, uid: decodedToken.uid });` — logs authenticated user's email AND uid. Confirmed verbatim.
- Line 101: `console.log('Translation request', { textLength: text.length, sourceLanguage, preview: text.substring(0, 50) });` — logs a 50-char preview of the input. Confirmed verbatim.
- Lines 143-148: `console.log('Translation successful', { translateDuration, totalDuration, resultLength: translatedText.length, preview: translatedText.substring(0, 50) });` — logs a 50-char preview of the translated output. Confirmed verbatim.
- Lines 154-158: full error message + stack logged server-side via console.error, while the client only receives the generic `{ error: 'Translation failed' }` at line 159. The finding's parenthetical claim (generic to client, detailed stack stays server-side) is accurate.

The data-sensitivity characterization is also correct: the translate function exists to translate the owner's private Danish->English conversation speech (config defaults source 'da', line 120; ALLOWED_EMAILS at lines 8-12 are the owner and partner), so the input/output previews are private message content, and email+uid are PII. These are written to GCP Cloud Logging via console.log/console.error, which persist by default.

One nuance the finding under-counts but does not get wrong: there are actually MORE email-logging sites than the single line 58 cited — line 61 (`Forbidden access attempt`, email), and line 73 (`Rate limit exceeded`, uid+email). These reinforce the same finding rather than contradicting it. Also worth noting the previews are intentionally truncated to 50 chars, which limits (but does not eliminate) content exposure — short messages are logged in full.

Severity 'info' is appropriate and well-justified by the finding itself: single-project owner, content already truncated, no exposure to third parties, and the client response is correctly sanitized. This is genuine data-minimization hygiene, not an exploitable vulnerability. The proposed fix (drop/gate the preview logs, decouple email from content logging, set a log retention policy) is correct and proportionate. Claim is accurate, evidence verbatim, severity and fix both sound. Confirmed.

surviving.title: AnimatedQuote re-creates its rAF loop on every position update (60fps)
surviving.description: The animation useEffect depends on [item, allItems, index, onUpdate]. Each rAF frame calls onUpdate -> setHypeData, producing new `item`/`allItems` references, so React tears down (cancelAnimationFrame) and re-runs (requestAnimationFrame) the effect on every committed render. With N quotes each updating ~60x/sec this thrashes effect setup/teardown, makes motion jerky (each item animates from a stale render snapshot), and wastes CPU. onUpdate/onBringToFront are also un-memoized, reinforcing the churn.
surviving.evidence: useEffect(() => { const animate = () => { ... onUpdate(index, x, y, vx, vy); animationRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(animate); }; animationRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(animate); return () => { if (animationRef.current) cancelAnimationFrame(animationRef.current); }; }, [item, allItems, index, onUpdate]); // allItems dependency ensures fresh data
surviving.recommendation: Run one shared rAF loop (or one per item with a stable empty dep array) that reads the latest positions from a ref and mutates them in place, writing to React state at a throttled cadence (e.g. via a ref-stored array plus an occasional setHypeData, or by mutating the DOM left/top directly through a node ref instead of going through state at all). Wrap updateItem and bringToFront in useCallback so onUpdate/onBringToFront are stable. With the loop decoupled from per-frame state churn, the O(N) effect teardown/setup and O(N) array rebuild every frame both disappear. A simpler interim mitigation: store positions in a ref and only call setHypeData on an interval (e.g. every ~50ms) so React renders at ~20fps while the rAF loop animates the refs/DOM at 60fps.
surviving.verifier_note:
The core mechanism is correct and I verified it against the actual file. The useEffect at AgenticAIWorkshop.tsx:39-99 has dep array `[item, allItems, index, onUpdate]` (line 99). Inside the rAF callback, line 91 calls `onUpdate(index, x, y, vx, vy)`, and `updateItem` (lines 160-164) does `setHypeData(prev => prev.map(...))`, returning a brand-new array plus a new object for the moved item every frame. Because each `AnimatedQuote` receives `item={hypeData[index]}` and `allItems={hypeData}` (lines 636-642), every committed render hands all children new `item`/`allItems` references, so React tears down (cancelAnimationFrame, line 97) and re-subscribes (requestAnimationFrame, line 95) the effect on essentially every frame. The un-memoization claim is also accurate: `updateItem` (160) and `bringToFront` (166) are recreated each render with no useCallback, so the `onUpdate` dep alone invalidates the effect for ALL items every render — not just the one that moved. The "stale render snapshot" point is real: line 41 destructures from the `item` prop closure and line 46 iterates the `allItems` prop closure, so each effect instance only sees the data from the render that created it (the author even acknowledges this with the comment on line 99 "allItems dependency ensures fresh data"). The proposed fix (single rAF loop mutating a ref + throttled state writes, or stable dep array reading latest from refs, plus useCallback on the handlers) is sound and idiomatic.

I mark partial only on severity. This is a genuine performance/jank bug, but it is confined to a single decorative word-cloud widget at the very bottom of a public marketing page (/agentic-ai-workshop), with no crash, data loss, incorrectness of user-facing data, or security impact. The visible symptom is CPU waste and jerky motion of the quote cloud. "High" overstates the blast radius for a non-critical animated component; medium is more accurate.

surviving.title: LovesIngy writes corrupt/NaN data to Firestore when a message is valid JSON
surviving.description: Any message that JSON.parses to an object/array enters the override branch, even though it only makes sense for the TRMNL ingest shape {text, timestamp}. Verified: `[1,2,3]` and `{"foo":"bar"}` both pass `typeof === 'object' && truthy`. parsedMessage.text is then undefined (written as message: undefined) and `new Timestamp(undefined/1000, undefined%1000)` = `new Timestamp(NaN, NaN)`, which throws or persists NaN seconds/nanos. That corrupts the orderBy('timestamp') read and the TRMNL sync that consumes these docs.
surviving.evidence: const parsedMessage = JSON.parse(newLoveMessage); if (parsedMessage && typeof parsedMessage === 'object') { messageData = { message: parsedMessage.text, timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp / 1000, parsedMessage.timestamp % 1000) }; }
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read src/components/LovesIngy/LovesIngy.tsx:147-189. The cited lines 161-172 match exactly. handleAddLoveMessage initializes messageData = {message: newLoveMessage, timestamp: serverTimestamp()}, then JSON.parses the input and, on line 164, overrides if `parsedMessage && typeof parsedMessage === 'object'`.

Verified the branch-entry claim with Node: `[1,2,3]` and `{"foo":"bar"}` both pass `typeof === 'object' && truthy` and yield `text=undefined`, `timestamp=undefined`. (Also `{}` enters; only `null`, numbers, strings, booleans fall through.) So line 166 writes `message: undefined` and line 167 computes `new Timestamp(undefined/1000, undefined%1000)` = `new Timestamp(NaN, NaN)`.

Verified against the actually-installed firebase 12.12.0 (package.json `^12.1.0`, resolved to 12.12.0): `new Timestamp(NaN, NaN)` does NOT throw in this version — it persists `seconds: NaN, nanoseconds: NaN`. So the corrupt doc is written to Firestore (the finding's "throws or persists NaN" hedge is correct; this version persists).

Verified the read-path impact, which is actually worse than the finding states: onSnapshot at line 59-68 maps every doc, and line 65 does `data.timestamp ? data.timestamp.toDate().toISOString() : ...`. The NaN Timestamp is truthy, so `.toDate()` runs and throws "Invalid time value" (confirmed via Node). That exception is thrown inside Array.map, so the entire snapshot mapping crashes and the whole message list fails to render — not just the one bad message. This corrupts the orderBy('timestamp','desc') view for ALL messages until the bad doc is removed.

The evidence snippet, file:line, mechanism, and proposed fix are all accurate. Caveat: triggering requires a user to type valid JSON into the love-message input of a private single-user app, so real-world likelihood is low (medium severity is defensible, arguably could be high given one bad doc bricks the whole list render, but low likelihood keeps it at medium).

surviving.title: LovesIngy onSnapshot error handler overwrites the permission-denied message
surviving.description: The generic 'Failed to load messages' is set unconditionally first, then the permission-denied message overrides it inside the if. It works only by ordering luck; the inverted structure is fragile compared to Progress.tsx:241-245 which uses a clean if/else.
surviving.evidence: setError('Failed to load messages. Please refresh the page.'); if (error.code === 'permission-denied') { setError('Access denied. You do not have permission to view these messages.'); }
surviving.recommendation: No behavioral fix is required; the current code is deterministically correct. As an optional readability/consistency cleanup, restructure to mirror Progress.tsx:240-247: `if (error.code === 'permission-denied') { setError('Access denied. You do not have permission to view these messages.'); } else { setError('Failed to load messages. Please refresh the page.'); }`. This removes the redundant unconditional setError. Reframe the finding as a style/consistency nit, not a bug — there is no "ordering luck" since both setError calls run synchronously in one handler invocation.
surviving.verifier_note:
The cited code is quoted accurately. LovesIngy.tsx:69-75 onSnapshot error handler does: line 71 `setError('Failed to load messages. Please refresh the page.')` unconditionally, then lines 72-74 `if (error.code === 'permission-denied') { setError('Access denied...') }`. Progress.tsx:240-247 indeed uses a clean if/else with the permission-denied branch first.

However, the finding mischaracterizes the mechanism. Both setError calls execute synchronously within a single handler invocation, so the second call deterministically wins when permission-denied is true and the first value is what remains otherwise. This is plain sequential JavaScript execution, not "ordering luck" — there is no race, async timing, or order-of-evaluation hazard. The handler produces functionally correct, deterministic output in both cases. So this is NOT a react-bug; it is a style/consistency nit (the double-set + override pattern is less readable than the if/else used in Progress.tsx, and the redundant first setError is a minor wasted state set within the same synchronous tick that React batches anyway).

The proposed fix (mirror Progress.tsx's if/else) is correct and a reasonable readability improvement, so the finding is partly valid. But the framing as fragile/lucky is wrong, and there is no observable defect. Severity low is acceptable but the dimension classification as a bug is overstated — it is closer to info-level cleanup.

surviving.title: Countdown for an event happening today renders "-0 days" and disagrees with TRMNL
surviving.description: getDaysUntil diffs eventDate at local midnight against `now` (later in the day), so a same-day event yields a small negative diffTime and Math.ceil returns -0. The list filter `>= 0` keeps it, and the UI renders '-0 days'. Meanwhile trmnl-sync.mjs:111 filters `e.days > 0`, dropping same-day events, so the in-app view and the device disagree on whether today's event appears.
surviving.evidence: const diffTime = eventDate.getTime() - now.getTime(); return Math.ceil(diffTime / (1000 * 60 * 60 * 24)); // verified -> -0 for today's date; rendered as `${days} days`
surviving.recommendation: Title/evidence are wrong: the UI renders "0 days" for a same-day event, never "-0 days" (template-literal coercion of -0 gives "0"). The real, minor issues are: (1) align the in-app filter at LovesIngy.tsx:274 (`>= 0`) with trmnl-sync.mjs:111 (`> 0`) so the app and device agree on whether today's event appears; (2) optionally normalize `now` to local midnight in getDaysUntil so same-day yields a clean +0 and special-case 0 to render "Today" instead of "0 days". The -0 itself is harmless because JS coerces it to "0" on render.
surviving.verifier_note:
I re-read LovesIngy.tsx:138-143 and 273-293 and scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs:90-114, and empirically tested the math in node.

CONFIRMED parts:
- getDaysUntil (lines 138-143) does diff eventDate at local midnight (`new Date(date + 'T00:00:00')`) against `now` (current time). For a same-day event when now is past midnight, diffTime is a small NEGATIVE fraction and Math.ceil returns -0. Verified: Object.is(value, -0) === true.
- The filter at line 274 uses `getDaysUntil(event.date) >= 0`, which keeps same-day events, while trmnl-sync.mjs:111 uses `.filter(e => e.days > 0)`, which drops them. So the in-app view and the TRMNL device genuinely disagree about whether today's event shows. This is real.

REFUTED part (the headline and core evidence):
- The finding claims the UI renders "-0 days". It does NOT. There is exactly one render path, line 292: `{`${days} days`}`. JavaScript template-literal/string coercion of -0 yields "0", not "-0". I verified: `\`${-0} days\`` === "0 days". The finding's own evidence comment ("verified -> -0 for today's date; rendered as ${days} days") conflates the internal numeric -0 with the rendered string. The user sees "0 days", which is reasonable UX, not a visible defect.

So the dramatic symptom in the title/evidence is factually wrong. A genuine but lesser issue remains: the >=0 vs >0 filter mismatch (app shows today, device hides it) and the lack of a "Today" label. Severity stays low: this is a single private app for one whitelisted user, cosmetic/consistency only, no functional breakage. Marking partial because the bug exists but is mis-described (no "-0 days" ever renders).

surviving.title: VisitsDenmark recreates SpeechRecognition on every online/offline event, interrupting dictation
surviving.description: queueTranslation's deps include isOnline, so its identity changes whenever the online/offline listeners fire. The speech-recognition setup effect lists queueTranslation in its deps, so a transient network blip (common on the stated mobile target) tears down the SpeechRecognition instance (recognition.stop()) and builds a new one mid-session. In-flight/buffered speech in the old onresult closure is lost and the onend->restart logic races with the just-issued stop().
surviving.evidence: queueTranslation = useCallback(..., [isOnline, processQueue]); ... useEffect(() => { const recognition = new SpeechRecognition(); ... return () => { recognition.stop(); recognitionRef.current = null; }; }, [language, queueTranslation]);
surviving.recommendation: Store the latest queueTranslation in a ref (as speechModeRef/languageRef already do) and call queueTranslationRef.current(...) inside onresult. Key the recognition effect on [language] only.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read src/components/VisitsDenmark/VisitsDenmark.tsx in full. Every load-bearing claim checks out against the actual code:

1) queueTranslation deps include isOnline — line 218: `}, [isOnline, processQueue]);`. Confirmed.
2) isOnline flips via the online/offline listeners — lines 97-106 (handleOnline/handleOffline call setIsOnline). A real online<->offline transition produces a new queueTranslation reference.
3) The speech-recognition setup effect depends on queueTranslation — line 313: `}, [language, queueTranslation]);`. Confirmed.
4) On that dep change the effect cleanup runs recognition.stop() (line 310) + nulls the ref (line 311), then a fresh `new SpeechRecognition()` is built (line 243) — i.e. the recognizer is torn down and recreated mid-session. Confirmed.

The race the finding describes is also real: recognition.stop() fires onend (line 290); since isListeningRef.current is still true, onend calls the OLD instance's recognition.start() (line 294), while the effect body's `if (isListeningRef.current) recognition.start()` (lines 305-307) starts the NEW instance — two starts racing, which the Web Speech API can reject ("already started"). At minimum dictation is interrupted with a gap.

The onresult handler (lines 248-281) and pending buffers are closed over / stored in refs (pendingTextRef, fastModeBufferRef persist across instances), so finalized+queued text is not lost; but in-flight interim text in the old closure and the listening continuity are disrupted.

One refinement to the description: the trigger is an actual isOnline state TRANSITION, not mere packet loss — setIsOnline(true) while already true is a React no-op and won't recreate the callback. But genuine offline->online transitions are common on the stated mobile target, so the practical impact holds. Severity medium is correct: intermittent, self-healing (the new instance restarts when listening), no persisted-data loss, but it does interrupt the core feature mid-use.

Proposed fix is correct and idiomatic: the file already uses exactly this pattern (speechModeRef/languageRef at lines 81-82 synced at 87-88, and onresult/onend already read .current). Adding a queueTranslationRef and keying the recognition effect on [language] only cleanly decouples the recognizer lifecycle from network state.

surviving.title: VisitsDenmark fires a test translation on mount that can send Bearer undefined
surviving.description: An empty-dep mount effect calls queueTranslation('Hej'/greeting), which calls getAuthToken -> user?.getIdToken(). If `user` is not yet populated when the view renders, the fetch goes out with `Authorization: Bearer undefined`, the cloud function rejects it, and a spurious error toast shows on first load. It also burns a real translation request on every mount. The eslint-disable hides the omitted user/language/queueTranslation deps.
surviving.evidence: useEffect(() => { const testText = language === 'da-DK' ? 'Hej' : '...'; queueTranslation(testText); // eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps }, []);
surviving.recommendation: Delete the mount effect at VisitsDenmark.tsx:227-232 entirely — it is leftover debug code that fires a real translateText cloud-function call and inserts a bogus history entry on every visit to /translate. There is no need to "gate it on user" because the route is already auth-gated (AuthProvider's loading gate + PrivateRoute's user check guarantee `user` is non-null before this component mounts), so the proposed "Bearer undefined" guard addresses a problem that does not exist. If a connectivity/auth smoke-check is genuinely wanted, it should be opt-in (e.g., behind a dev-only flag) rather than running unconditionally in production.
surviving.verifier_note:
The finding's headline mechanism ("Bearer undefined" / spurious error toast on first load because `user` isn't populated yet) is REFUTED by the actual auth gating, but the secondary observation (a real test translation fires on every mount) is TRUE.

What the code actually shows:
- src/components/VisitsDenmark/VisitsDenmark.tsx:228-232 — an empty-dep mount effect calls `queueTranslation('Hej' | 'नमस्ते')`. Confirmed verbatim.
- queueTranslation (196-218) -> processQueue (125-194) -> getAuthToken (113-123) which does `const token = await user?.getIdToken()` and sends `Authorization: Bearer ${token}` (147).

Why "Bearer undefined" is unreachable on this route:
- VisitsDenmark is only mounted via PrivateRoute (router.tsx:75-83).
- AuthContext.tsx:38-40: while `loading` is true, AuthProvider renders `<Loading "Authenticating...">` and does NOT render its children at all. main.tsx:10-12 confirms AuthProvider wraps the entire RouterProvider, so nothing under the router renders during the async onAuthStateChanged resolution.
- PrivateRoute.tsx:9: only renders children when `user` is truthy; otherwise it redirects to /login.
Therefore by the time VisitsDenmark's mount effect runs, `loading === false` AND `user` is non-null. `user?.getIdToken()` returns a real token, not undefined. The described "Bearer undefined + spurious error toast on first load" path cannot occur. (Even an expired/revoked token would make getIdToken() reject -> caught by processQueue's try/catch and re-queued, still never literally "Bearer undefined".)

What IS real: lines 228-232 are leftover debug/test code that unconditionally fires a genuine cloud-function translation request on every mount of /translate, prepending a junk "Hej"->translation entry to the history each visit. That wastes a real API call and pollutes UX. The eslint-disable claim is only partly right: `user` is not referenced in the effect body so it isn't an "omitted user dep" — only `language` and `queueTranslation` are referenced and intentionally omitted for run-once-on-mount.

Net: severity is correct (low) but the rationale is wrong; the real issue is "leftover test translation on mount," not an auth/Bearer-undefined bug.

surviving.title: VisitsDenmark caches auth token for 58 min with no refresh on 401/403, causing a retry loop
surviving.description: The token is cached for a self-chosen 58-minute TTL rather than the token's real exp, and the response handler only special-cases 429. A 401/403 from an expired/revoked token throws and re-queues the item (unshift), but nothing invalidates tokenCacheRef, so the next attempt reuses the same stale cached token and the item retries forever. Firebase ID tokens expire after ~1h; clock skew or force-revoke makes the 58-min window stale.
surviving.evidence: if (tokenCacheRef.current && tokenCacheRef.current.expiry > now) return tokenCacheRef.current.token; ... if (response.status === 429) { ... } throw new Error(errorData.error || 'Translation failed'); ... translationQueueRef.current.unshift(item);
surviving.recommendation:
Two coordinated changes:

1) Backend (functions/src/index.ts): stop collapsing auth-verification failures into 500. Wrap verifyIdToken (line 57) in its own try/catch and return 401 on failure (e.g. err.code === 'auth/id-token-expired' or 'auth/id-token-revoked' -> 401). Without this, the client cannot tell an expired token from a real backend error.

2) Client (VisitsDenmark.tsx): in processQueue's response handling (around lines 154-160), special-case 401/403 in addition to 429: on 401, clear tokenCacheRef.current = null and retry once with a force-refreshed token via user.getIdToken(true) before re-queueing. Critically, also add an attempt counter to each queue item (e.g. {text, timestamp, attempts}) and stop re-queueing (line 184) once attempts exceeds a small cap (e.g. 3), surfacing a terminal error instead of looping. Independently, add backoff on the error re-trigger path (lines 190-192) — currently only the success path waits QUEUE_DELAY_MS, so failures spin tightly with zero delay. These two client changes (attempt cap + error-path backoff) are the real fix; the 401/403 special-casing alone is insufficient because expired tokens currently arrive as 500.
surviving.verifier_note:
The structural bug is real and verified, but the finding mis-describes the trigger mechanism and proposes a fix that wouldn't work against the very case it targets.

VERIFIED parts (src/components/VisitsDenmark/VisitsDenmark.tsx):
- Token cached with a self-chosen 58-min TTL, not the token's real exp: TOKEN_CACHE_DURATION_MS = 58*60*1000 (line 54); getAuthToken returns the cached token while expiry > now (lines 115-116) and stamps expiry = now + TTL (line 120). Confirmed.
- Response handler only special-cases 429 (lines 156-159); everything else throws a generic Error (line 159). Confirmed.
- On any throw, the catch block re-queues the item via translationQueueRef.current.unshift(item) (line 184), and tokenCacheRef is never cleared anywhere in the catch (173-184) or finally (185-193). Confirmed — the stale token is reused on the next attempt.
- The loop is real and is actually a TIGHT loop, which the finding under-states: the finally block sets isProcessingRef.current = false (188) and immediately calls processQueue() again if queue.length > 0 (190-192). The QUEUE_DELAY_MS await (line 172) is ONLY on the success path, so a persistently-failing item retries with no backoff at all — CPU spin + hammering the cloud function until it self-inflicts a 429 from the backend's own 30/min rate limit (functions/src/index.ts:72-75).

WRONG part — the trigger codes 401/403:
I read the backend (functions/src/index.ts). An expired or revoked Firebase ID token does NOT produce 401 or 403. admin.auth().verifyIdToken(token) at line 57 THROWS on an expired/revoked token; that throw is caught by the outer catch at line 152 and returns status 500 with {error:'Translation failed'}. The 401 (lines 50-53) fires only for a missing/malformed Bearer header; the 403 (lines 60-63) fires only for a valid token whose email is not in ALLOWED_EMAILS. So the finding's premise "A 401/403 from an expired/revoked token" is factually incorrect about this code path.

This also breaks the proposed fix: special-casing 401/403 to force-refresh would never trigger for the expired-token scenario, because that scenario surfaces as 500. The client literally cannot distinguish "token expired" from "translation backend errored" given the current backend, which collapses both into 500.

Net: real bug (no token invalidation + no attempt cap + tight no-backoff retry), wrong mechanism description, and an incomplete fix. Hence partial. Severity medium is fair: it is the auth-gated /translate route (router.tsx:75-79), single/few users, no data loss, but it can spin and rate-limit-lock the user out.

surviving.title: Login whitelist check runs after Firebase sign-in; PrivateRoute admits any signed-in user during the gap
surviving.description: ALLOWED_EMAILS is enforced client-side only AFTER Firebase Auth has already signed the user in, so onAuthStateChanged in AuthContext sets a valid `user`. signOut(auth) is called but not awaited (line 27), and PrivateRoute treats ANY truthy `user` as authorized regardless of email. Between the redirect/popup resolving and signOut completing, navigating to /progress or /lovesingy renders protected content. Server-side Firestore rules still gate real data, but the client authorization model is wrong.
surviving.evidence: if (userEmail && !ALLOWED_EMAILS.includes(userEmail)) { signOut(auth); alert(...); return; } ... PrivateRoute: return user ? children : <Navigate to="/login" .../>;
surviving.recommendation: Centralize the whitelist in AuthContext. In AuthContext.tsx onAuthStateChanged (line 30-33), check the email: if a user is present but user.email is not in ALLOWED_EMAILS, call signOut(auth) and setCurrentUser(null) instead of setting the user. Then PrivateRoute (relying on useAuth) automatically only admits allowed users, and Login.tsx no longer needs to do its own post-sign-in check (though the non-awaited signOut on line 26 should still be made `await`/`return signOut(...)` and the alert moved into a .then to avoid the blocking-alert-before-signOut ordering). Note this is defense-in-depth only — the real authorization boundary is the Firestore security rules, which already restrict data access to the same whitelisted emails and remain unchanged.
surviving.verifier_note:
All factual claims verified against the actual files. AuthContext.tsx:30-33 sets currentUser to whatever Firebase reports with zero email filtering. PrivateRoute.tsx:9 returns `user ? children : <Navigate to="/login" .../>` with no email check, and useAuth.ts:4 just passes the context through. The whitelist (ALLOWED_EMAILS, config.ts) is enforced ONLY in Login.tsx — after Firebase sign-in completes (popup line 48-50, redirect line 21-25). Line 26 `signOut(auth);` is indeed NOT awaited in the redirect branch (contrast line 51 `await signOut(auth)` in the popup branch). So the authorization model is genuinely client-side and after-the-fact: any signed-in user (Firebase persists sessions across reloads) is treated as authorized by PrivateRoute until signOut propagates. A non-allowed user who is briefly signed in can render the protected component shell (/progress, /lovesingy) during that window. The bug and the proposed fix (enforce whitelist in AuthContext.onAuthStateChanged + signOut + treat as unauthenticated; await signOut in Login) are both correct.

Where the finding is mis-scoped: severity. The finding rates medium but its own description concedes the real data is gated server-side by Firestore rules (element-tracker = admin only; love-ingy/trmnl = 3 whitelisted emails — and that whitelist is the SAME set as ALLOWED_EMAILS). So a non-allowed user cannot actually read private data; they can at most see the empty/erroring protected UI shell transiently before signOut fires. The phrase "renders protected content" overstates impact since the load-bearing security boundary (Firestore rules) stays intact and unchanged. Impact is bounded to a cosmetic/transient UI authorization gap, which is low rather than medium. The defense-in-depth point (client authz model is wrong) is valid and worth fixing, hence not rejected.

surviving.title: "Yesterday" and streak-start dates use Date.now() - 86400000, wrong on DST days
surviving.description: Subtracting a fixed 86,400,000 ms is not 'one calendar day earlier' across DST boundaries. On spring-forward/fall-back days, `now - 86400000ms` can land on the same calendar date or skip to two days prior depending on local hour, so getLocalDateString() returns the wrong YYYY-MM-DD. This makes the Yesterday nudge target the wrong Firestore doc and mis-counts the streak start on ~2 days/year. (trmnl-sync.mjs uses a proper Eastern-time conversion; the in-app code uses raw ms math.)
surviving.evidence: const yesterdayDate = new Date(Date.now() - 86400000); ... const startDate = entries.has(todayStr) ? new Date(today) : new Date(Date.now() - 86400000);
surviving.recommendation: Replace both `new Date(Date.now() - 86400000)` sites with calendar arithmetic. For line 211-212: `const yesterdayDate = new Date(); yesterdayDate.setDate(yesterdayDate.getDate() - 1); const yesterdayDateString = getLocalDateString(yesterdayDate);`. For line 90: compute yesterday the same way, e.g. `const yd = new Date(today); yd.setDate(yd.getDate() - 1); const startDate = entries.has(todayStr) ? new Date(today) : yd;`. This matches the existing getDateNDaysAgo helper (lines 39-43) and is DST-safe (verified: in America/New_York, setDate(-1) from 2026-03-09 yields 2026-03-08, whereas the ms subtraction yields 2026-03-07).
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read src/components/Progress/Progress.tsx. Both cited lines exist exactly as claimed: line 90 `const startDate = entries.has(todayStr) ? new Date(today) : new Date(Date.now() - 86400000);` and line 211 `const yesterdayDate = new Date(Date.now() - 86400000);`. getLocalDateString (lines 25-30) formats using local getFullYear/getMonth/getDate, so the literal 24h ms subtraction is not equivalent to "one calendar day earlier" on a DST transition day.

I reproduced the bug. Using Intl in America/New_York: at the morning after spring-forward (March 9, 2026, 00:30 EDT = 2026-03-09T04:30Z), `now - 86400000ms` resolves to local date 2026-03-07, i.e. TWO days prior, not the true yesterday 2026-03-08. The 23-hour spring-forward day gets skipped because a literal 24h is subtracted. Impact is real at both sites: (1) yesterdayDateString (line 212) feeds the Firestore doc target (line 359 `date: yesterdayDateString`), the displayed nudge date (lines 708/728 formatDateForDisplay(yesterdayDate)), and the yesterdayEntry lookup (line 213) — so the "Yesterday" section would target/show two-days-ago; (2) the startDate at line 90 (only when today is unlogged) makes calculateStreak begin from the wrong day, under-counting or zeroing the streak.

I verified the proposed fix is correct and DST-safe: with TZ=America/New_York, `d = new Date(now); d.setDate(d.getDate() - 1)` from March 9 yields March 8 (correct), because setDate uses local calendar arithmetic. It is also consistent with the existing getDateNDaysAgo helper (lines 39-43), which already does `d.setDate(d.getDate() - n)`. The known-good trmnl-sync.mjs comparison is fair context.

Two minor corrections to the description, neither of which undermines the finding: (a) the failure direction is specifically spring-forward ("skip to two days prior"); the fall-back ("same calendar date") scenario does not reproduce in the normal early-morning case (Nov 2 00:30 EST correctly gives Nov 1), so the "same calendar date" half of the mechanism is over-stated; (b) the trigger window is narrower than "~2 days/year" — effectively the ~1-hour early-morning window on the single spring-forward day. "low" severity is appropriate: single-user app, self-correcting (effect disappears after ~01:00 local or once the day passes), no data corruption beyond writing/showing the wrong date if the user logs during that narrow window.

surviving.title: Streak milestone side effects run inside the setEntries updater (impure, double-fires in StrictMode)
surviving.description: The setEntries updater function performs side effects: celebrateStreak() (confetti), setLastCelebratedStreak, and sessionStorage.setItem. React may invoke an updater twice under StrictMode/concurrent rendering, and the app is wrapped in <React.StrictMode> in main.tsx, so confetti can double-fire. Also lastCelebratedStreak is captured from the enclosing render closure rather than read fresh, so rapid successive saves compare against a stale value.
surviving.evidence: setEntries((prevEntries) => { const newEntries = new Map(prevEntries); newEntries.set(todayDateString, {...}); const newStreak = calculateStreak(newEntries); if (STREAK_MILESTONES.includes(newStreak) && lastCelebratedStreak !== newStreak) { celebrateStreak(); setLastCelebratedStreak(newStreak); sessionStorage.setItem('lastCelebratedStreak', newStreak.toString()); } return newEntries; });
surviving.recommendation: Move streak/milestone computation out of the updater: after the awaited setDoc, build the next map, compute the streak, and run the side effects once in handler flow (or a useEffect keyed on entries). Keep the updater pure.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read the cited code and every factual claim holds.

1. Impure updater confirmed twice: src/components/Progress/Progress.tsx:294-318 (handleSave) and 407-431 (handleConfirmSave). The setEntries((prevEntries) => {...}) updater calls celebrateStreak() (confetti; defined lines 152-159), setLastCelebratedStreak(newStreak) (line 313/426), and sessionStorage.setItem('lastCelebratedStreak', ...) (line 314/427) — all side effects inside what must be a pure state-reducer. This violates React's rule that updater functions be pure.

2. StrictMode confirmed at src/main.tsx:9 (<React.StrictMode> wraps the whole app). React double-invokes updater functions under StrictMode, so the confetti + sessionStorage write fire twice. Mechanically the guard does NOT prevent the second fire: both invocations start from the same prevEntries and the same closure-captured lastCelebratedStreak (still stale on the synchronous second invoke), so STREAK_MILESTONES.includes(newStreak) && lastCelebratedStreak !== newStreak passes both times.

3. Stale closure confirmed: lastCelebratedStreak is state (declared lines 200-203) read inside the updater (lines 310, 423) from the enclosing render closure rather than fresh, so rapid successive saves compare against a stale value.

The one correction to scope (not severity): React StrictMode's double-invocation of state updaters is a DEVELOPMENT-ONLY behavior; it does not occur in the production gh-pages build. So the "confetti double-fires" symptom is observable in `npm run dev` but not for the deployed end user. Combined with this being a single-user tracker with one entry/day (milestone collisions within one render are near-impossible) and the effect being purely cosmetic (an extra confetti burst), the proposed `low` severity is correct — keep it. The proposed fix (compute next map + streak after the awaited setDoc and run side effects once in handler flow, keeping the updater pure) is the correct React idiom and applies to both call sites.

surviving.title: LovesIngy deleteCountdownEvent uses filtered-array index against the unfiltered array, deleting the wrong event
surviving.description: The rendered list filters out past events (`.filter(event => getDaysUntil(event.date) >= 0)`) then maps with `index` being the position in the FILTERED array. But deleteCountdownEvent(index) runs `countdownEvents.filter((_, i) => i !== index)` against the UNFILTERED countdownEvents. If any earlier event has already passed and was filtered out, the displayed index no longer matches the source index, so clicking delete removes a different (wrong) event.
surviving.evidence: countdownEvents.filter(event => getDaysUntil(event.date) >= 0).map((event, index) => (... onClick={() => deleteCountdownEvent(index)} ...)) ... const updatedEvents = countdownEvents.filter((_, i) => i !== index);
surviving.recommendation: Carry the original index through the filter (e.g. map to {event, origIndex} before filtering) and pass origIndex to deleteCountdownEvent, or delete by identity (matching emoji+name+date) rather than by positional index.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read src/components/LovesIngy/LovesIngy.tsx in full. The bug is real.

Render path (lines 273-301): countdownEvents.filter(event => getDaysUntil(event.date) >= 0).map((event, index) => ...). The `index` bound at line 275 is the position in the FILTERED array. Line 296: onClick={() => deleteCountdownEvent(index)} passes that filtered index.

Delete path (lines 131-136): deleteCountdownEvent runs countdownEvents.filter((_, i) => i !== index) against the UNFILTERED countdownEvents state (line 47).

The filter at line 274 drops events with getDaysUntil < 0 (past events). getDaysUntil (lines 138-143) returns negative for past dates. Crucially, addCountdownEvent sorts ascending by date (lines 122-124), so any elapsed/past events occupy the LOWEST indices of the stored array. The display filter removes exactly those low-index entries, so filtered index N maps to unfiltered index N + (count of past events).

Concrete trace: countdownEvents = [PastA(0), FutureB(1), FutureC(2)] (date-sorted). Filtered display = [FutureB(filtered idx 0), FutureC(filtered idx 1)]. Clicking delete on FutureB calls deleteCountdownEvent(0), which filters out countdownEvents[0] = PastA. The user deletes the wrong event (and FutureB remains). This is silent data corruption on a delete action.

No alternative delete path, data-index attribute, or identity-based mapping exists to correct it — the positional index is the only thing passed. The claim's evidence, root cause, and proposed fix (carry origIndex through the filter, or delete by identity matching emoji+name+date) are all correct.

Severity medium is appropriate and I leave it unchanged: it is a genuine wrong-data-deletion bug, but the route (/lovesingy) is a private app gated to 3 whitelisted emails, the impact is recoverable (re-add the deleted countdown, and the data also lives in trmnl-config/countdowns), and it only manifests once at least one countdown has elapsed into the past. Not high (no security/availability/broad-user impact), not low (it does delete the wrong user data when triggered).

surviving.title: Index-based React keys in reordered/rebuilt lists (word cloud, countdowns)
surviving.description: AnimatedQuote items use `key={index}` while hypeData is rebuilt every frame and reordered by bringToFront; index keys defeat reconciliation and can desync per-item hover/front state. The countdown list also uses `key={index}` on a filtered array, compounding the delete-by-index bug above.
surviving.evidence: hypeData.map((item, index) => <AnimatedQuote key={index} ...>) ; countdownEvents.filter(...).map((event, index) => <div key={index} className="countdown-item">)
surviving.recommendation: Drop the word-cloud (AgenticAIWorkshop.tsx:635-644) claim entirely: the array is never reordered, inserted into, or deleted from at runtime, so key={index} is harmless there. For the countdown list (LovesIngy.tsx:279), the meaningful fix is not the key per se but to stop using array index as the delete identity: derive a stable key from event fields (e.g. `${event.emoji}-${event.name}-${event.date}`) AND change deleteCountdownEvent to delete by matching that identity against countdownEvents rather than by the filtered render index, which fixes the real (separately-tracked) delete-by-index defect. The key change alone is cosmetic without that.
surviving.verifier_note: Both cited code lines exist exactly as claimed: AgenticAIWorkshop.tsx:635-644 has `hypeData.map((item, index) => <AnimatedQuote key={index} .../>)`, and LovesIngy.tsx:279 has `key={index}` inside `countdownEvents.filter(...).map((event, index) => ...)`. However the finding's core rationale for the word cloud is factually wrong. The claim "hypeData is rebuilt every frame and reordered by bringToFront" is half false: hypeData IS rebuilt each frame (updateItem at lines 160-164 is called from the rAF loop at line 91), but neither updateItem nor bringToFront reorders the array. updateItem uses `prev.map((item,i) => i===index ? {...item,...} : item)` and bringToFront (lines 166-170) uses `prev.map((item,i) => ({...item, isFront: i===index}))` — both preserve array order and only mutate fields/identity by position. hypeData is populated once from CSV (line 218) and never has items inserted, deleted, or moved at runtime. With a stable-order list, key={index} is functionally equivalent to a stable id, so the claimed reconciliation desync of per-item hover state (local isHovered, line 37) and front state (item.isFront, derived by index) does NOT occur. The word-cloud concern is therefore not a real bug. The countdown half is a weaker but valid code smell: key={index} uses the FILTERED index (line 275), and on the date-sorted (lines 122-124) date-filtered (line 274) list the keys shift when an item is deleted. But the genuine defect there is the already-separately-tracked delete-by-index bug (deleteCountdownEvent at line 131-136 filters the UNFILTERED countdownEvents by the filtered index), not the key itself. So the finding is partly true (code present, minor key smell on countdowns) but mis-scoped: its primary justification for the higher-impact word-cloud case is incorrect. Severity low is acceptable; the word-cloud item should be dropped/downgraded to info since it is a non-issue given stable array order.

surviving.title: RedirectToExternal navigates as a render side effect
surviving.description: window.location.href is assigned directly in the render body, which is an impure side effect during render and is disallowed (re-renders or StrictMode double-render run it again). The sibling redirect components (WifeRedirect, ResumeRedirect) correctly do this inside useEffect. The component is not currently referenced by router.tsx, so impact is latent dead code, but it will misbehave if reused.
surviving.evidence: const RedirectToExternal = ({ url }) => { window.location.href = url; return null; };
surviving.recommendation: Move the assignment into useEffect(() => { window.location.href = url; }, [url]) like the other redirect components, or delete the file if it is dead code.
surviving.verifier_note:
All claims verified against the actual file contents. src/components/RedirectToExternal.tsx line 8 assigns `window.location.href = url;` directly in the render body of the function component (the body returns null on line 9), which is an impure side effect during render — re-renders and React 18 StrictMode's intentional double-render in dev will execute it again. The claimed evidence matches the real code (the only deviation is the typing: it is `React.FC<RedirectToExternalProps>`, not the slightly simplified `({ url }) =>` shown, which is immaterial).

The two supporting claims also hold: WifeRedirect.tsx (lines 4-6) and ResumeRedirect.tsx (lines 4-6) both correctly wrap `window.location.href = ...` inside `useEffect(() => { ... }, [])`, so the report's contrast with sibling components is accurate.

Dead-code claim verified: `grep -rn "RedirectToExternal" src/` returns only references inside RedirectToExternal.tsx itself (the interface, the component, the export). It is imported/used nowhere, including router.tsx, so current production impact is nil and the finding's "latent" framing is correct.

Severity low is appropriate: real anti-pattern but no live impact since the component is unused. The proposed fix (move assignment into `useEffect(() => { window.location.href = url; }, [url])` to match siblings, or delete the dead file) is correct and idiomatic.

surviving.title: 'pnpm lint' break is stale node_modules (ESLint 8), not a config mismatch — fixed by reinstall
surviving.description: The prior 'No files matching the pattern src' error is NOT an eslint flat-config vs lint-script mismatch. The lockfile correctly resolves eslint@9.39.4, but the installed node_modules/eslint was a stale 8.57.1 copy left over from before the pnpm migration (dated Sep 12; .bin symlink also stale). ESLint 8 ignores eslint.config.ts (flat config) and its CLI rejects the bare 'src' glob, producing that error. ESLint 9 reads eslint.config.ts via jiti and resolves 'src' fine. I reproduced the failure, then pnpm relinked the bin on direct invocation and 'pnpm lint' now passes cleanly (exit 0, ESLint 9.39.4, flat config loaded, src linted). Implication: the script 'eslint src --max-warnings 0' and eslint.config.ts ARE mutually compatible; CI (which runs 'pnpm install --frozen-lockfile' fresh) would lint correctly. The breakage is purely the developer's stale local tree.
surviving.evidence: $ pnpm lint -> 'ESLint: 8.57.1 ... No files matching the pattern "src"'. node_modules/eslint/package.json version: 8.57.1 (mtime Sep 12). Lockfile: eslint@9.39.4. After relink: node_modules/.bin/eslint --version -> v9.39.4 and 'pnpm lint' exit 0.
surviving.recommendation: Run 'rm -rf node_modules && pnpm install' (or 'pnpm install --force') to sync node_modules to the lockfile. No config change needed. Optionally add a lint job to deploy.yml to catch this in CI. The eslint.config.ts and package.json lint script are correct as-is.
surviving.verifier_note:
I re-read the cited files and reproduced the current state.

package.json:17 is exactly `"lint": "eslint src --max-warnings 0"`. jiti@2.6.1 is present in devDependencies (line 50), which is what lets ESLint 9 load a TS flat config. eslint.config.ts (1-31) is a valid flat config: default-exports an array with an `ignores` object and a `files: ['**/*.{ts,tsx}']` block — exactly the shape ESLint 9's flat-config loader expects and ESLint 8 ignores.

The finding's core claim — that the prior "flat-config vs lint-script mismatch" diagnosis is wrong and the real cause was a stale node_modules — is directly supported:
- pnpm-lock.yaml resolves eslint to 9.39.4 (line 1007; referenced as eslint@9.39.4(jiti@2.6.1) throughout). There is no eslint@8.x entry resolved for the project's own dep — node_modules/.pnpm contains only `eslint@9.39.4_jiti@2.6.1`.
- node_modules/eslint/package.json reports "version": "9.39.4", and node_modules/.bin/eslint --version prints v9.39.4.
- I ran `pnpm lint` and it exited 0 with no errors — i.e., `eslint src` + eslint.config.ts are mutually compatible. This refutes the original "config mismatch" theory: a lockfile-synced tree lints cleanly with no config change.
- The "No files matching the pattern src" symptom is a known ESLint 8 behavior (ESLint 8 doesn't read eslint.config.ts by default and its CLI rejects bare directory args without an `--ext`), consistent with the finding's explanation that a leftover 8.x copy caused it.

I could not independently re-observe the historical 8.57.1 stale copy because node_modules has since been fully reinstalled (every eslint-related path is mtime Jun 9 16:06, today — the post-relink state the finding describes). But the diagnosis is internally consistent, the cited lockfile/config evidence holds, and the corrective conclusion is proven by the now-passing run.

The proposed fix (`rm -rf node_modules && pnpm install`, no config change) is correct and matches reality. I verified the CI claim too: .github/workflows/deploy.yml (the only workflow with build steps) has no lint step — it goes install -> functions npm ci/build -> build -> deploy — so the optional suggestion to add a lint job to CI is valid and would catch this. Severity medium is appropriate: a broken local `pnpm lint` (which is also a pre-commit hook per package.json:20-23) is a real DX/quality-gate breakage, but it does not affect the production build or CI deploy and is fixed by a reinstall.
