surviving.title: protobufjs transitive chain has 1 critical + 4 high CVEs in the production tree (firebase)
surviving.description: pnpm audit (verified, not the stale '39 Dependabot' figure) reports 16 total advisories: 1 critical, 7 high, 8 moderate. The dominant cluster is protobufjs@7.5.4, pulled transitively through firebase>@firebase/firestore>@grpc/proto-loader. This is a PRODUCTION dependency and accounts for the critical (arbitrary code execution, <7.5.5), 4 highs (code injection/code-gen gadget/process-wide DoS/unbounded recursion), and 5 moderates. @grpc/proto-loader accepts protobufjs ^7.5.5, and patched releases 7.5.8/7.5.9 exist on the 7.x line, so this is fixable without a major bump.
surviving.evidence: pnpm audit --json: [CRITICAL] protobufjs (<7.5.5) path .>firebase>@firebase/firestore>@grpc/proto-loader>protobufjs; resolved protobufjs@7.5.4 in pnpm-lock.yaml; npm view protobufjs versions shows 7.5.8, 7.5.9 exist; @grpc/proto-loader deps: protobufjs ^7.5.5.
surviving.recommendation: Primary fix is correct and verified: add a pnpm override in package.json — "pnpm": { "overrides": { "protobufjs": ">=7.5.9" } } — then run `pnpm install` and re-run `pnpm audit --prod` to confirm the protobufjs cluster (1 critical + 4 high + 4 moderate) clears. Note: @grpc/proto-loader@0.7.15 declares protobufjs ^7.2.5 (not ^7.5.5 as the finding states), so >=7.5.9 is comfortably in range. Secondary note: prefer the override over bumping firebase alone — firebase latest is 12.14.0, but a firebase bump only helps if it pulls a @grpc/proto-loader whose resolved protobufjs is >=7.5.9; the override is the deterministic fix. Also be aware one protobufjs moderate (CVE-2026-45740) is vulnerable <=7.5.7, so an override of >=7.5.8 or higher is required to clear everything — the proposed >=7.5.9 satisfies this.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read package.json and pnpm-lock.yaml and ran the actual pnpm audit. Every load-bearing claim checks out:

- package.json:28 is "firebase": "^12.1.0" (verified, exact).
- pnpm-lock.yaml resolves protobufjs@7.5.4 (lines 1546/3451) via the chain firebase@12.12.0 -> @firebase/firestore@4.14.0 -> @grpc/proto-loader@0.7.15 -> protobufjs@7.5.4 (confirmed in lockfile lines 2228-2236 and 2419-2423, and echoed verbatim by the audit "paths" field ".>firebase>@firebase/firestore>@grpc/proto-loader>protobufjs"). This is a real PRODUCTION dependency — firestore is used by the app's auth-gated pages.
- pnpm audit --json reports exactly 16 advisories: 1 critical, 7 high, 8 moderate (matches metadata.vulnerabilities {info:0, low:0, moderate:8, high:7, critical:1}). The stale "39 Dependabot" figure is correctly set aside.
- The critical is CVE-2026-41242 (GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg), CVSS 9.8, "Arbitrary code execution in protobufjs", vulnerable <7.5.5 — exactly as claimed.
- The 4 highs are all protobufjs and match the descriptions: code-generation gadget after prototype pollution (CVE-2026-44291), code injection via bytes field defaults (CVE-2026-44293), process-wide DoS via unsafe option paths (CVE-2026-44290), and unbounded protobuf recursion DoS (CVE-2026-44289).
- Patched versions exist on the 7.x line: npm shows 7.5.5, 7.5.6, 7.5.7, 7.5.8, 7.5.9 published, latest-7 = 7.6.2. So fixable without a major bump, as claimed.
- No pnpm.overrides block currently exists in package.json, so the proposed override is net-new and valid.

Severity "high" is appropriate: a CVSS 9.8 arbitrary-code-execution CVE is in the resolved production tree. (Real-world exploitability is gated on loading attacker-controlled protobuf schemas, which Firestore's client does not do with untrusted input, so this is high rather than escalated to critical for THIS app — the finding already chose high, which is the right call.)

Two immaterial inaccuracies that do not change the verdict: (1) The finding says the cluster "accounts for ... 5 moderates" — the audit shows 4 protobufjs moderates (CVE-2026-44294, 44292, 44288, 45740), not 5; the other moderates are postcss etc. (2) The finding says "@grpc/proto-loader accepts protobufjs ^7.5.5" — its actual declared range is the looser ^7.2.5, which only makes the override safer. Neither affects the fix or severity.

surviving.title: react-router production dep is one minor behind two HIGH advisories (open redirect / DoS)
surviving.description: react-router@7.14.0 is resolved in the lockfile (via react-router-dom), but the audit flags 2 highs and 1 moderate: vendored turbo-stream arbitrary-content RCE-style issue (patched >=7.14.2), DoS via unbounded path expansion (patched >=7.15.0), and open redirect via protocol-relative '//' paths (patched >=7.14.1). This is a prod dependency on auth-gated SPA routes. Latest react-router-dom is 7.17.0.
surviving.evidence: pnpm audit: [HIGH] react-router (>=7.0.0 <7.15.0 -> >=7.15.0) DoS via unbounded path expansion; [HIGH] react-router (<=7.14.1 -> >=7.14.2) turbo-stream; resolved react-router@7.14.0; npm view react-router-dom version -> 7.17.0.
surviving.recommendation: Bump react-router-dom to 7.17.0 (the existing ^7.14.0 caret already permits it — verified semver-satisfies and peer deps met), via `pnpm update react-router-dom` (optionally also raise the floor to ^7.17.0 in package.json:35), then commit the updated pnpm-lock.yaml. Treat this as routine dependency hygiene, not an urgent security fix: all three flagged react-router advisories (turbo-stream RCE GHSA-49rj-9fvp-4h2h, __manifest DoS GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78, open-redirect GHSA-2j2x-hqr9-3h42) only trigger in React Router Framework Mode / SSR, and this app is a pure client-side SPA using createBrowserRouter on static GitHub Pages (no react-router server, no __manifest endpoint, no server redirect() — confirmed in src/router.tsx and via grep), so none are exploitable here.
surviving.verifier_note:
Version facts are all accurate and re-verified. package.json:35 declares react-router-dom "^7.14.0"; pnpm-lock.yaml lines 1570 and 1577 resolve react-router-dom@7.14.0 AND react-router@7.14.0; npm shows latest react-router-dom is 7.17.0; and `pnpm audit` does flag exactly three react-router advisories. So the "one minor behind, lockfile resolves 7.14.0" framing is real.

However the finding is mis-scoped on severity and, more importantly, on exploitability:

1) SEVERITY ERROR. The title says "two HIGH advisories (open redirect / DoS)". Per `pnpm audit --json`, the open-redirect advisory (GHSA-2j2x-hqr9-3h42 / CVE-2026-40181, patched >=7.14.1) is MODERATE, not high. The two genuine HIGHs are the turbo-stream RCE (GHSA-49rj-9fvp-4h2h, patched >=7.14.2) and the __manifest DoS (GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78, patched >=7.15.0). So one of the two advisories the title calls HIGH is actually moderate.

2) EXPLOITABILITY OVERSTATED — the decisive issue. All three advisory overviews (read from audit JSON) explicitly scope the bug to React Router *Framework Mode* (an SSR server): the RCE overview says "When using React Router v7 in Framework Mode... requires the application code to have an existing prototype pollution vulnerability"; the DoS targets the server-only "__manifest endpoint" in "Framework Mode applications"; the open-redirect concerns the server-side `redirect` data API. I re-read src/router.tsx: the app uses createBrowserRouter (Library/Data mode) as a pure client-side SPA (router.tsx:21), with client-side <Navigate> only (router.tsx:142, PrivateRoute.tsx:9). grep across src/ and functions/src/ found ZERO framework-mode/SSR usage: no @react-router/dev|node|serve deps (package.json + functions/package.json), no loader:/action: data functions, no server redirect(), no turbo-stream usage. The app ships as static files on GitHub Pages — there is no react-router server, no __manifest endpoint, no Framework Mode. Therefore none of the three vulns has a realistic exploit path in this deployment. The finding's "prod dependency on auth-gated SPA routes" point is true but irrelevant to whether these CVEs can fire.

The fix is correct and low-risk: I confirmed semver ^7.14.0 satisfies 7.17.0, and react-router-dom@7.17.0 peer deps (react >=18, node >=20) are met by this repo (react 19.2.5, engines node >=20.19.0). A `pnpm update react-router-dom` is free and worth doing for hygiene. But the high severity and "two HIGH advisories" title misrepresent real risk for a client-only static SPA.

surviving.title: deploy.yml is half-migrated: pnpm for app install but npm for functions + global firebase-tools
surviving.description: The app install uses 'pnpm install --frozen-lockfile' (line 37) and pnpm/action-setup@v4, but: (a) functions still use 'cd functions && npm ci' (line 40) and 'npm run build' (line 43) against functions/package-lock.json — a separate, un-migrated package manager; (b) the app build step calls 'npm run build' (line 46) instead of 'pnpm run build', which works only because npm falls back to the script but bypasses pnpm; (c) 'npm install -g firebase-tools' (line 61) installs an unpinned global on every run, slow and non-reproducible. Also the entire workflow is gated only on workflow_dispatch (lines 3-11) — there is no automated deploy on push, and no lint/typecheck gate at all.
surviving.evidence: Line 37: 'pnpm install --frozen-lockfile'; line 40: 'cd functions && npm ci'; line 46: 'run: npm run build'; line 61: 'npm install -g firebase-tools'.
surviving.recommendation: Either fully migrate functions to pnpm (delete functions/package-lock.json, add functions to a pnpm workspace or run 'pnpm --dir functions install --frozen-lockfile' + 'pnpm --dir functions run build') or explicitly document functions stays on npm. Change line 46 to 'pnpm run build'. Pin firebase-tools (e.g. 'pnpm dlx firebase-tools@^14 ...' or add it as a devDependency) instead of an unpinned global install. Add a 'pnpm lint' + 'tsc' step before build.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read .github/workflows/deploy.yml line-by-line; every factual claim holds. Line 37: `run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`. Line 27: `uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4`. Line 40: `run: cd functions && npm ci`. Line 43: `run: cd functions && npm run build`. Line 46: `run: npm run build` (app build) — not `pnpm run build`. Line 61: `npm install -g firebase-tools` (unpinned global). Lines 10-11 gate the workflow on `workflow_dispatch` only; lines 4-8 show the push/PR triggers commented out, so there is no automated deploy and no lint/typecheck step anywhere in the file.

Corroborating evidence: `functions/package-lock.json` exists (102 KB) and `functions/package.json` has its own `build: tsc` script, confirming functions remain on un-migrated npm with a separate lockfile. The repo root has `pnpm-lock.yaml` (127 KB) and no root `package-lock.json`, and there is NO `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, so functions are not part of any pnpm workspace — supporting the proposed fix. The root `package.json` `predeploy` script already uses `pnpm run build`, which makes line 46's `npm run build` an internal inconsistency, strengthening sub-claim (b).

One minor imprecision in the description: sub-claim (b) says line 46 "works only because npm falls back to the script but bypasses pnpm." Mechanically, `npm run build` simply invokes the script runner (`tsc && vite build`) against the already-installed pnpm `node_modules`; it works fine and is a consistency smell rather than a functional break. This does not undermine the finding's substance.

Severity: this is CI/CD hygiene on a manually-triggered (workflow_dispatch), single-maintainer personal site — no production runtime impact and no security exposure. That argues toward the low end. But the issues compound (dual lockfiles to keep in sync, unpinned global firebase-tools = non-reproducible builds, mixed package managers, no lint/typecheck gate before deploy), so medium is defensible. Keeping severity unchanged at medium. The proposed fix is concrete and correct.

surviving.title: functions/ still on npm (package-lock.json committed) while repo migrated to pnpm — version skew
surviving.description: The repo migrated to pnpm (root pnpm-lock.yaml, pnpm-builds.json) but functions/ retains a committed npm package-lock.json and has no node_modules installed. functions/package.json also has skew: it declares firebase-admin ^12.0.0, but the trmnl-sync workflow and known-fixed sync script require firebase-admin v14's modular API — so the Functions runtime (admin v12) and the GH Actions sync script (admin v14) are on different major versions of the same SDK with incompatible export shapes. Additionally functions engines.node is '22' but @types/node is ^20.0.0, so the build types against an older Node than it runs on.
surviving.evidence: functions/package-lock.json is git-tracked; functions/package.json: firebase-admin ^12.0.0, engines.node '22', @types/node ^20.0.0; trmnl-sync.yml line 27 pins firebase-admin@^14.
surviving.recommendation: Treat this as package-manager consistency drift, not a runtime version conflict. functions/ legitimately runs an isolated admin v12 (namespace import in functions/src/index.ts:2) and the GH Actions sync script (scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs) runs an isolated admin v14 (modular imports) — these are separate runtimes with no shared module resolution, so they do NOT conflict and no admin alignment is required for correctness. The only real cleanup items: (1) decide whether functions/ stays on npm; if intentional, add a one-line note to CLAUDE.md so the committed functions/package-lock.json reads as deliberate rather than missed migration (this largely overlaps the existing deploy.yml half-migration finding — consider folding it in). (2) Optionally bump functions @types/node to ^22 to match engines.node "22" (functions/package.json:21) for type accuracy, though this is cosmetic since the build already succeeds. Do NOT bump functions firebase-admin to ^14 solely to "match the sync script" — there is no shared context that needs matching, and a major SDK bump in the deployed Functions runtime is a far riskier change than the cosmetic drift it would resolve.
surviving.verifier_note:
I re-read all cited files. The factual claims check out, but the load-bearing "version skew / incompatible export shapes" framing is wrong, so this is partial (over-scoped severity + incorrect mechanism).

VERIFIED FACTS:
- functions/package-lock.json IS git-tracked (`git ls-files` confirms), root has pnpm-lock.yaml + pnpm-builds.json tracked, no functions/pnpm-lock.yaml. So functions/ is on npm while root is on pnpm. TRUE.
- functions/package.json:16 declares `"firebase-admin": "^12.0.0"`; lockfile (functions/package-lock.json:1137) resolves it to 12.7.0. TRUE.
- functions/package.json:11-13 engines.node "22"; :21 @types/node "^20.0.0". TRUE.
- .github/workflows/trmnl-sync.yml:27 is `run: npm install firebase-admin@^14`. TRUE.
- functions/node_modules absent locally — but this is EXPECTED, not evidence: functions/.gitignore lists `node_modules/`. Its local absence is normal and proves nothing about breakage.

WHY THE SEVERITY/MECHANISM IS WRONG:
The finding claims the functions runtime (admin v12) and the GH Actions sync script (admin v14) are "different major versions of the same SDK with incompatible export shapes," implying a real conflict. They are NOT in any shared context:
- scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs runs in GitHub Actions, does its own `npm install firebase-admin@^14`, and uses MODULAR subpath imports (`import { initializeApp, cert } from 'firebase-admin/app'`; `import { getFirestore } from 'firebase-admin/firestore'`) — explicitly written to be v14-compatible (its own header comment documents the v14 migration).
- functions/src/index.ts runs in the Firebase Functions runtime and uses `import * as admin from 'firebase-admin'` against admin v12.
These two never share a node_modules, lockfile, or import graph — the sync script does not import from functions/ and vice versa. admin v12 + namespace import works; admin v14 + modular import works. There is no "incompatible export shape" interaction; the export removal that broke things only ever affected the sync script (already fixed, per known findings). So the central justification for medium severity does not exist.

The genuinely true residual is purely organizational: functions/ uses npm while the repo migrated to pnpm. That is real but is consistency drift, not a functional defect, and it substantially overlaps the already-known deploy.yml half-migration finding (which already flags functions still using npm). The @types/node ^20 vs engines.node "22" mismatch is real but benign (older Node types are largely forward-compatible; the build succeeds per known findings) — info-level at most. Net: downgrade to low and drop the false "incompatible export shapes / version skew between runtimes" mechanism.

surviving.title: 'pre-commit' hook is configured two ways but installed neither way — it does not run
surviving.description: There are TWO competing pre-commit mechanisms and neither is active. (1) package.json has a 'pre-commit' field listing ['lint','build'] plus a devDependency on the npm 'pre-commit' package — but that package installs its git hook only on 'npm install', and this repo now installs via pnpm, so the hook was never (re)installed. (2) .pre-commit-config.yaml configures the Python pre-commit framework whose hooks call 'npm run lint' / 'npm run build' (npm, not pnpm). Checking .git/hooks shows only 'pre-commit.old' — no active 'pre-commit' hook exists. So commits are NOT gated by lint or build despite the configuration implying they are. Both configs also reference npm, conflicting with the pnpm migration.
surviving.evidence: .git/hooks/ contains only 'pre-commit.old' (no 'pre-commit'). package.json devDependencies has 'pre-commit': '^1.2.2' (the npm hook installer, also the source of the cross-spawn ReDoS high CVE). .pre-commit-config.yaml entry: 'npm run lint' and 'npm run build'.
surviving.recommendation: Pick one. If using the Python pre-commit framework: run 'pre-commit install', change entries to 'pnpm run lint'/'pnpm run build', and drop the npm 'pre-commit' package + package.json 'pre-commit' field. If using the npm 'pre-commit' package: add a pnpm postinstall to register the hook and keep the package.json field. Removing the npm 'pre-commit' package also eliminates the cross-spawn high-severity advisory.
surviving.verifier_note:
Every concrete claim is supported by the actual files I re-read.

1. package.json:20-23 contains `"pre-commit": ["lint", "build"]`, and devDependencies lists `"pre-commit": "^1.2.2"` (line 51). This is the npm hook-installer package, which registers its git hook via its own install/postinstall step that runs on `npm install`. The repo is now on pnpm (confirmed by `predeploy: "pnpm run build"` at line 11), so that hook was never re-registered under pnpm.

2. .pre-commit-config.yaml configures the Python pre-commit framework with a `local` repo and two hooks whose `entry` is `npm run lint` and `npm run build` (npm, not pnpm) — verified lines 1-14. This is a second, competing mechanism.

3. .git/hooks/ contains ONLY `pre-commit.old` (no active `pre-commit` hook), plus the standard `.sample` files. `core.hooksPath` is unset. `pre-commit.old` is the renamed npm-pre-commit installer hook (`./node_modules/pre-commit/hook`). So neither mechanism is active: the Python framework's `pre-commit install` was never run, and the npm package's hook was renamed to `.old` and never re-created. Commits are genuinely not gated by lint or build.

4. The cross-spawn link is correct: pnpm-lock.yaml:3441-3445 shows `pre-commit@1.2.2` depends on `cross-spawn: 5.1.0` (resolution at lines 911-912). cross-spawn 5.1.0 falls in the vulnerable `<6.0.6` range of the ReDoS advisory (GHSA-3xgq-45jj-v275 / CVE-2024-21538), so dropping the `pre-commit` package would indeed eliminate that transitive advisory.

The proposed fix (pick one mechanism, switch entries to pnpm, drop the npm `pre-commit` package + package.json field, and either run `pre-commit install` or add a pnpm hook-registration step) is correct and complete. Severity medium is appropriate: this is a CI/quality-gate gap plus a transitive high CVE pulled in only by a dead tool — not a production-runtime defect, but more than cosmetic.

surviving.title: deps/ holds 12 committed Vite pre-bundle cache artifacts, referenced nowhere
surviving.description: The tracked deps/ directory is a Vite dependency pre-bundling cache: _metadata.json contains optimized-chunk hashes ('hash','browserHash','optimized' map), and the files are pre-bundled copies of react/react-dom/react-router-dom plus a package.json with only {"type":"module"}. It is git-tracked (git ls-files confirms 10+ files) but referenced nowhere in src/, index.html, vite.config.ts, or package.json. This is regenerated build cache that belongs in .vite/, which .gitignore already excludes ('**/.vite/**'). It is pure dead weight and can confuse contributors into thinking it is a vendored dependency.
surviving.evidence: git ls-files deps/ -> deps/_metadata.json, deps/chunk-QSQYAWSL.js, deps/react.js, deps/react-router-dom.js, etc. grep 'deps/' across src/ index.html vite.config.ts package.json -> no matches. deps/_metadata.json begins with Vite optimizer hash metadata.
surviving.recommendation: `git rm -r deps/` is correct and sufficient. The .gitignore part is optional and should be applied with care: do NOT add a bare `deps/` pattern (it would silently ignore any future legitimately-named top-level source folder). Since Vite's real cache lives at node_modules/.vite/deps (already covered transitively by `node_modules` on line 10 and `**/.vite/**` on line 14), no new ignore rule is actually needed. If a guard is desired, scope it as `/deps/` (repo-root only) rather than `deps/`. After removal, run `pnpm build` to confirm dist/ still builds (it will — nothing imports deps/).
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read every cited file and confirmed all claims against actual contents.

EVIDENCE:
1. `git ls-files deps/` returns exactly 12 tracked files: deps/_metadata.json, deps/chunk-QSQYAWSL.js(+.map), deps/package.json, deps/react.js(+.map), deps/react-dom_client.js(+.map), deps/react-router-dom.js(+.map), deps/react_jsx-dev-runtime.js(+.map). The "12 committed" count is exact.

2. deps/_metadata.json (lines 1-35) is unmistakably a Vite optimizer cache: top-level "hash":"5f2c87b4", "browserHash":"d1708824", an "optimized" map keyed by react/react-dom/react-router-dom with "src":"../../node_modules/..." paths, "fileHash", "needsInterop", and a "chunks" map pointing to chunk-QSQYAWSL.js. This is the canonical Vite _metadata.json shape.

3. deps/react.js (lines 1-5) is a pre-bundled re-export shim: `import { require_react } from "./chunk-QSQYAWSL.js"; export default require_react();` with a sourceMappingURL. Pure build artifact, not vendored source.

4. deps/package.json is exactly {"type":"module"} — the marker Vite drops into its deps cache dir.

5. grep for "deps/" across src/, index.html, vite.config.ts, package.json returned ZERO matches. A broader grep across all *.ts/*.js/*.mjs/*.json/*.yml/*.html found references ONLY inside docs/audit-workflow.mjs (the audit's own task description) — nothing in the real build/runtime path. So "referenced nowhere" holds.

6. vite.config.ts (read in full) does not configure optimizeDeps.cacheDir or build.cacheDir, so this top-level deps/ is not even Vite's active cache location (Vite defaults to node_modules/.vite/deps). It is a stray relocated/committed copy — consistent with the finding's "stray copy" hypothesis.

7. .gitignore line 14 is `**/.vite/**`, confirming the cache-exclusion claim and explaining why a top-level deps/ (not under .vite/) slipped through and got committed.

Build does not reference deps/ (no import/asset path points at it). Severity "low" is appropriate: it is committed dead weight (~3.3 MB of .js/.map per ls -la) that can confuse contributors, but causes no build/runtime/security failure.

surviving.title: Deprecated GH Actions: checkout@v4 / setup-node@v4 (Node 20 runtime) and gh-pages@v3
surviving.description: Both workflows pin actions/checkout@v4 and actions/setup-node@v4, which run on the deprecated Node 20 action runtime (GitHub is moving the default to Node 24; v5 of both actions uses the current runtime). deploy.yml also uses peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3, which is several majors behind (v4 is current). These still work but emit deprecation warnings and will eventually fail when GitHub sunsets the Node 20 runner.
surviving.evidence: deploy.yml:19 'uses: actions/checkout@v4'; :22 'actions/setup-node@v4'; :70 'peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3'. trmnl-sync.yml:17,21 same checkout/setup-node@v4.
surviving.recommendation: Bump action versions in both workflows: deploy.yml lines 19/22 and trmnl-sync.yml lines 17/20 to actions/checkout@v5 and actions/setup-node@v5; deploy.yml line 70 to peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v4. Add `cache: pnpm` to the setup-node step in deploy.yml ONLY (it uses pnpm via pnpm/action-setup@v4 and `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`); place pnpm/action-setup BEFORE setup-node so the cache key resolves. Do NOT add `cache: pnpm` (or `cache: npm`) to trmnl-sync.yml — that job does a bare `npm install firebase-admin@^14` with no lockfile, so a cache directive there has nothing to key on and adds no value. This is a low-urgency housekeeping change: deploy.yml runs only on manual workflow_dispatch (push/PR triggers are commented out), so the deprecation warnings rarely surface.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read both cited files. The factual claims are accurate. deploy.yml:19 is `uses: actions/checkout@v4`, :22 is `uses: actions/setup-node@v4`, :70 is `uses: peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3` — all three exactly as claimed. trmnl-sync.yml:17 is `uses: actions/checkout@v4` (correct). One minor evidence error: the finding cites trmnl-sync.yml:21 for setup-node@v4, but line 21 is actually `with:`; setup-node@v4 is on line 20. Trivial off-by-one.

The technical substance is correct: actions/checkout@v4 and setup-node@v4 run on the Node 20 action runtime; v5 of both moved to Node 24, and GitHub does emit deprecation warnings as it transitions the default runner. peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3 is indeed behind v4. So the finding is real, not hallucinated.

However, severity should be downgraded from low to info, and parts of the fix are off:
1) Impact is minimal here. deploy.yml only triggers on `workflow_dispatch` (line 10-11, push/PR triggers are commented out lines 4-8), so it runs rarely and manually. These are deprecation warnings, not failures — "will eventually fail" is speculative; GitHub has historically given long runways and auto-bumped node runtimes.
2) The proposed fix's "add 'cache: pnpm' to speed installs" is good for deploy.yml (it uses pnpm via pnpm/action-setup@v4 on line 26-29). But it is WRONG for trmnl-sync.yml: that workflow does NOT use pnpm — it runs `npm install firebase-admin@^14` (line 27) with no lockfile, so `cache: pnpm` would break (no pnpm-lock context for that job) and `cache: npm` would also be pointless/error-prone since there's no package-lock.json driving that bare install. The fix should not blanket-apply cache:pnpm to both files.
3) The fix correctly omits, but worth noting, that pnpm/action-setup@v4 (deploy.yml:27) is current and fine.

Net: real but mis-scoped severity and a fix that is partially incorrect for trmnl-sync.yml.

surviving.title: 879 KB single JS chunk (247 KB gzip), no code-splitting — Vite itself warns
surviving.description: The production build emits one 794-879 KB main chunk (~247 KB gzip) because rollupOptions defines no manualChunks/code-splitting; firebase (full SDK) + react-router + react-spring + papaparse + match-sorter + react-activity-calendar all land in one bundle. Vite 8 prints the >500 kB warning and explicitly suggests dynamic import() and codeSplitting. Auth-gated routes (/progress, /lovesingy, /translate) and the static landing page all download the whole bundle up front. Separately, dist ships a 1.35 MB 404.gif to the client, dwarfing the JS — worth optimizing too.
surviving.evidence: vite build output: 'dist/assets/index-Deq4f_QE.js 794.90 kB │ gzip: 246.87 kB' plus '(!) Some chunks are larger than 500 kB ... Consider Using dynamic import()'. dist/assets/404-*.gif 1,347.51 kB.
surviving.recommendation: Add code-splitting; drop the already-satisfied firebase-import step and re-scope the gif fix. (1) Lazy-load route components in src/router.tsx with React.lazy + Suspense, splitting the auth-gated routes (Progress, LovesIngy, VisitsDenmark) off the static landing page — this is the highest-leverage change for the common landing-page payload. (2) Add build.rolldownOptions.output.manualChunks (Vite 8 / Rolldown; not rollupOptions) in vite.config.ts to split firebase and other vendor libs (react-router, react-spring, papaparse, match-sorter, react-activity-calendar) into separate chunks. (3) DO NOT change firebase import style — src/firebase.ts already imports per-module (firebase/app, firebase/auth, firebase/firestore) with zero umbrella imports. (4) Optionally compress/replace src/images/404.gif (1.35 MB), but note it loads only on the 404/error-boundary path (NotFound.tsx, ErrorBoundary.tsx), not on normal routes, so it is a low priority and does not affect the landing-page payload.
surviving.verifier_note:
Central claim verified against real files. vite.config.ts:37-45 has build.rollupOptions containing ONLY the copy() plugin — no manualChunks/output/codeSplitting. src/router.tsx:1-19 statically imports all 19 components (no React.lazy/Suspense), so auth-gated routes (Progress, LovesIngy, VisitsDenmark) bundle with the landing page. A fresh `npx vite build` reproduces the cited numbers exactly: dist/assets/index-Deq4f_QE.js 794.90 kB / gzip 246.87 kB, plus dist/assets/404-DIjrbEzU.gif 1,347.51 kB, and Vite does print "(!) Some chunks are larger than 500 kB after minification. Consider: Using dynamic import() to code-split...". So the chunk size, the missing code-splitting, and the warning are all genuine.

However the finding is mis-scoped in two ways, hence partial:

1. Proposed fix is partly already-done. It says "Import firebase per-module (firebase/firestore, firebase/auth) rather than the umbrella package." But src/firebase.ts already imports strictly per-module (firebase/app, firebase/auth, firebase/firestore on lines 2-4, plus a dynamic import('firebase/firestore') on line 39), and a grep for `from 'firebase'` umbrella imports across src/ returns zero hits. That fix step is moot and should be dropped — firebase bundle weight has to be addressed via manualChunks, not by changing import style.

2. The 404.gif framing is misleading. The finding says "dist ships a 1.35 MB 404.gif to the client, dwarfing the JS." The gif (src/images/404.gif, 1,347,512 bytes) is imported only by NotFound.tsx:2 and ErrorBoundary.tsx:2 — i.e. it loads on the 404 / error-boundary path, NOT on the landing page or normal routes. Vite hashes it as a separate asset, so a normal visitor never downloads it. It does not "dwarf the JS" on any normal page load; the JS chunk is the only thing that loads on every route. Worth optimizing, but it is not a routine client payload.

Minor: the task preamble called this "Vite 6"; the real package.json pins "vite": "^8.0.8" and the build banner is v8.0.8, so the finding's "Vite 8" reference is actually correct, and the warning's suggestion is build.rolldownOptions.output.codeSplitting (Rolldown), not rollupOptions.

Severity low is correct and unchanged: this is a single-user personal site, 247 KB gzip is heavy-ish but not pathological, no measured user impact, and the heaviest asset (gif) is off the hot path.

surviving.title: Reported '39 Dependabot vulnerabilities' is stale — actual pnpm audit is 16 (13 prod), concentrated in ~5 roots
surviving.description: The 39-vuln Dependabot figure predates the pnpm migration and overcounts. surviving.verifier_note:
I re-ran the audits and read package.json myself. The finding's headline numbers are exactly correct: `pnpm audit` = 16 (8 moderate / 7 high / 1 critical) and `pnpm audit --prod` = 13 (6 moderate / 6 high / 1 critical) — both match the claimed totals precisely. The 39 figure is the old npm/Dependabot count; the pnpm-lock.yaml reality is 16/13.

Root-cause attribution (verified via `pnpm audit --json` paths):

- @protobufjs/utf8: 1 advisory (prod, moderate) via the same protobufjs subtree.

package.json confirms `pre-commit: ^1.2.2` is a real npm devDependency (line 51) and `react-router-dom: ^7.14.0` (line 35), so the proposed remediation packages exist.

Two immaterial detail errors: the finding lumps protobufjs as "10 advisories" but it is actually 9 protobufjs + 1 separately-named `@protobufjs/utf8` (same project family, same fix). And the distinct vulnerable-package count is 6, not 5 — though they collapse to ~5 remediation actions since the two protobuf packages share one upgrade. Neither error touches the thesis or the fix plan: applying the protobufjs override and the react-router-dom bump plus removing `pre-commit` does clear the critical, all prod highs, and the cross-spawn high. Severity 'info' is appropriate (this is a backlog-accuracy/triage note, not a code defect). surviving.title: Remove tracked Vite pre-bundle cache committed at deps/
surviving.description: The deps/ directory holds 12 tracked files (3.2 MB on disk) that are a Vite dependency optimizer pre-bundle cache, the contents normally found in node_modules/.vite/deps. Evidence: deps/_metadata.json contains the optimizer hash/browserHash and an 'optimized' map pointing at '../../node_modules/react/index.js'; deps/package.json is just {"type":"module"}; deps/react.js is generated glue importing './chunk-QSQYAWSL.js'. surviving.title: translateText has no maxInstances/cost cap — unbounded billing exposure under abuse
surviving.description: translateText is declared with `functions.https.onRequest(...)` and there is NO runtime configuration anywhere in functions/src (verified: grep for region|runWith|setGlobalOptions|maxInstances|memory|timeoutSeconds returns nothing). With firebase-functions v7 / Gen 2 defaults, the function can scale up to the default concurrency/instance ceiling with no `maxInstances` cap. The function calls the *paid* Google Cloud Translation API (billed per character) on every accepted request. Although requests require a Firebase ID token AND a whitelisted email (index.ts:50-64), the only throttle is an in-memory rate limiter (see separate finding) that does not hold across instances. A compromised/abused whitelisted account, or simply a runaway client loop, could fan out across many instances and run up Translation API + Functions invocation cost with no ceiling.
surviving.evidence: export const translateText = functions.https.onRequest(async (req, res) => { ... }  // no region/runWith/maxInstances anywhere in functions/src
surviving.recommendation: This is a Gen 1 / v1 function (`import * as functions from 'firebase-functions'`; `functions.https.onRequest` at index.ts:22), so `setGlobalOptions` will NOT work. Use the v1 runtime-options builder: replace line 22 with `export const translateText = functions.runWith({ maxInstances: 3, timeoutSeconds: 30, memory: '256MB' }).https.onRequest(async (req, res) => {`. (Note v1 uses '256MB', not the v2 '256MiB'.) Alternatively, migrate to Gen 2 by importing `{ onRequest }` from 'firebase-functions/v2/https' and `{ setGlobalOptions }` from 'firebase-functions/v2', then call `setGlobalOptions({ maxInstances: 3, region: 'us-central1', timeoutSeconds: 30, memory: '256MiB' })` — but that is a larger change. A low maxInstances bounds worst-case cost and makes the per-instance rate limiter effective. As a backstop independent of code, set a Google Cloud billing budget alert and consider restricting the Translation API key.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read functions/src/index.ts in full and grepped the functions source. The factual core of the finding is confirmed:
- Line 22: `export const translateText = functions.https.onRequest(async (req, res) => {` is the only function declaration.
- grep -rnE "region|runWith|setGlobalOptions|maxInstances|memory|timeoutSeconds" over functions/src returns nothing — no runtime/instance cap is set. CONFIRMED.
- Lines 49-64: requests require a Firebase ID token (admin.auth().verifyIdToken) AND the decoded email must be in ALLOWED_EMAILS (3 hard-coded gmail addresses). CONFIRMED.
- Lines 18-20, 66-91: the rate limiter is a module-scope `new Map()` (30 req/min per uid). It is per-instance, so it does not bound aggregate load across multiple instances. CONFIRMED.
- Lines 113-125: every accepted request calls the paid Google Cloud Translation API (translation.googleapis.com, billed per character). CONFIRMED.

So the central claim — no maxInstances cap, paid API called per request, per-instance rate limiter that doesn't hold across instances — is real and verifiable.

Two corrections, hence PARTIAL not confirmed:

1) GENERATION MISLABELED. The finding says "firebase-functions v7 / Gen 2 defaults" and proposes `setGlobalOptions(...)`. But the code uses the v1 namespace API: `import * as functions from 'firebase-functions'` (line 1) and `functions.https.onRequest` (line 22). That is the Gen 1 / v1 API. Gen 2 requires `import { onRequest } from 'firebase-functions/v2/https'`. `setGlobalOptions` is a v2-only API and will NOT apply caps to this v1 function — so the proposed fix as written is wrong for this code. (package.json pins firebase-functions ^7.0.2, node 22; the version is fine, but the API surface used is v1.) The "no cap" conclusion still holds either way — Gen 1's default ceiling is in fact higher than Gen 2's — but the explanation and the fix are technically incorrect.

2) SEVERITY OVERSTATED. The endpoint is NOT publicly invocable: an attacker needs a valid Firebase ID token for one of exactly 3 whitelisted Google accounts (lines 60-64). The only realistic abuse paths the finding itself names are "a compromised whitelisted account" or "a runaway client loop" — both require either account compromise or a client-side bug, not open-internet abuse. There is also a working per-instance rate limiter (30/min) that bounds a single client against a single instance. Given the strong auth gate, this is a cost-hygiene / defense-in-depth gap rather than a directly externally-exploitable high-severity vulnerability. Medium is the appropriate severity.

surviving.title: Rate limiter is per-instance in-memory Map — ineffective across Cloud Function instances and cold starts
surviving.description: The rate limiter is `const rateLimiter = new Map<string, number[]>()` declared in module scope (index.ts:18, RATE_LIMIT_MAX=30/min at line 20). Cloud Functions run multiple concurrent instances; each instance has its own Map. A client distributing requests across N warm instances effectively gets ~30*N req/min, and every cold start resets the counter to empty. So the documented '30 requests per minute' guarantee is not enforced globally. Combined with the missing maxInstances cap, the per-character Google Translate billing is only loosely bounded. This is a soft limit at best, not a real abuse control.
surviving.evidence: const rateLimiter = new Map<string, number[]>(); // module scope, per-instance; RATE_LIMIT_MAX = 30; const userRequests = rateLimiter.get(uid) || [];
surviving.recommendation: Add a maxInstances cap so the in-memory limiter becomes approximately correct. Cleanest path: migrate this export to firebase-functions v2 (the package is already firebase-functions ^7) and set instance limits, e.g. `import { onRequest } from 'firebase-functions/v2/https'; export const translateText = onRequest({ maxInstances: 3, region: 'us-central1' }, async (req, res) => {...})`. With a low maxInstances the per-instance Map covers nearly all traffic for the 3-user whitelist, and it also hard-bounds the per-character Google Translate billing. If true cross-instance correctness is ever required, move the timestamp state to a Firestore collection keyed by uid (read-modify-write in a transaction) instead of the in-memory Map. Given the auth + 3-email allowlist already gating all access, the maxInstances cap is the proportionate fix.
surviving.verifier_note: Re-read functions/src/index.ts in full. Every concrete code claim checks out: line 18 `const rateLimiter = new Map<string, number[]>()` is at module scope; line 20 `RATE_LIMIT_MAX = 30 // 30 requests per minute`; line 69 `const userRequests = rateLimiter.get(uid) || []`; the function is the v1 `functions.https.onRequest` export at line 22. I grepped the entire functions/ tree for maxInstances/runWith/setGlobalOptions/concurrency and found NOTHING — so the "missing maxInstances cap" claim is verified, and v1 onRequest defaults to no instance cap. The technical mechanism is also correct: a module-scope Map in Cloud Functions lives per-instance, is wiped on cold start, and is not shared across concurrent warm instances, so the in-code comment "30 requests per minute" is not a global guarantee — a client spread across N warm instances can get ~30*N/min, and cold starts reset the counter. The finding is real and accurately described. I mark it PARTIAL only to correct severity. Access is gated upstream by Firebase Auth token verification (lines 50-57) AND a hardcoded 3-email allowlist (lines 8-12, enforced at 60-64) BEFORE the rate limiter is reached (line 67). So the only actors who can ever exercise this weak limiter are 3 trusted whitelisted users; there is no anonymous/public abuse surface. The realistic worst case is one of 3 trusted people inflating the per-character Google Translate bill — a cost/abuse concern, not a security boundary breach. Given that, medium overstates it; low is proportionate. The proposed fix is correct and sensible: option (a) — add a maxInstances cap (e.g. 1-3) which makes the in-memory limiter approximately meaningful — is the right-sized mitigation for a 3-user tool, and option (b) (Firestore/Redis state) is the correct heavier alternative if true global enforcement is ever needed.

surviving.title: No input size/type validation on `text` — large payloads inflate Translation API billing and can be abused
surviving.description: After auth, the function only checks `if (!text)` (index.ts:95). It does not validate that `text` is a string, nor cap its length before forwarding to Google Translate (`q: text` at line 119). Google Cloud Translation v2 is billed per character, so a single accepted request with a multi-megabyte string is billed in full. `text.length` is logged (line 101) and `text.substring` is called (line 101) — both assume a string; if a client sends `text` as a number/array/object, `.substring` throws and falls through to the generic 500 handler (still after the auth check, so low security impact, but it's an unhandled-shape bug). There is also no cap on requests batching huge inputs to multiply cost.
surviving.evidence: const { text, sourceLanguage } = req.body; if (!text) { ... } ... q: text, source: sourceLanguage || 'da'  // no length/type check, billed per char
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read functions/src/index.ts in full. The substantive claims are all accurate:
- Line 94: `const { text, sourceLanguage } = req.body;` — confirmed.
- Line 95: `if (!text)` is the ONLY guard — confirmed. No `typeof` check, no length cap.
- Line 101: `console.log(... textLength: text.length, preview: text.substring(0, 50))` — both assume `text` is a string — confirmed.
- Line 119: `q: text` is forwarded verbatim to Google Translate v2 (line 113-125) — confirmed.
Google Cloud Translation v2 is billed per character, so an oversized accepted string is billed in full. The type-shape bug is also real: if a client sends `text` as a number/object, `text.substring` throws a TypeError caught by the generic handler at line 152, returning a 500 (the `.length` access on a number yields `undefined` rather than throwing, but the finding correctly pins the throw on `.substring`). I confirmed the client (src/components/VisitsDenmark/VisitsDenmark.tsx:142-149, 248-270) sends short speech-recognition transcript chunks accumulated to a word threshold — so a small server-side length cap is well-aligned with real usage, validating the fix's design intent.

Why PARTIAL rather than confirmed: the severity is overstated. The finding frames this as billing abuse but under-weights two existing mitigations in the same file that bound the blast radius hard: (1) auth is restricted to a 3-email hardcoded allowlist (lines 8-12, enforced at line 60), so this is NOT an anonymous/internet-facing abuse vector — only 3 trusted users can even reach line 94; and (2) a per-uid rate limiter caps 30 requests/minute (lines 17-20, 66-91). With a tiny trusted user set plus a request cap, the realistic worst case is a trusted user (or a compromised/buggy client) inflating cost within 30 req/min — a robustness/defense-in-depth gap, not an exploitable medium-severity abuse path. The finding's own text even concedes "low security impact." So this is a legitimate low-severity input-validation hardening item.

The proposed fix is correct and idiomatic; it preserves the existing empty-string rejection (`text.length === 0`) and adds the missing `typeof` and length-cap checks. The 5000-char cap is a reasonable assumption given the verified sentence-by-sentence client usage.

surviving.title: `data.data.translations[0].translatedText` assumes Google API success shape — throws on unexpected response
surviving.description: After `if (!response.ok)` (line 130), the code does `const translatedText = data.data.translations[0].translatedText` (line 140) and immediately `translatedText.length` / `.substring` (line 146-147). This assumes the Google Translate response always has `data.data.translations[0].translatedText`. An HTTP 200 with an unexpected/empty body (e.g. `{}`, partial proxy response, or a future API change) makes `data.data` undefined and throws a TypeError, which is caught by the outer try/catch (line 152) and returned as a generic 500 'Translation failed'. Functionally tolerable (it doesn't crash the instance), but it masks the real cause and the success path has no defensive parsing.
surviving.evidence: const translatedText = data.data.translations[0].translatedText; ... resultLength: translatedText.length, preview: translatedText.substring(0, 50)
surviving.recommendation: Guard the parse: `const translatedText = data?.data?.translations?.[0]?.translatedText; if (typeof translatedText !== 'string') { console.error('Unexpected translate response', data); res.status(502).json({ error: 'Unexpected translation response' }); return; }`
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read functions/src/index.ts in full. Every cited detail matches the actual code:
- Line 130: `if (!response.ok)` — correct.
- Line 140: `const translatedText = data.data.translations[0].translatedText;` — exact match.
- Lines 146-147: `resultLength: translatedText.length` and `preview: translatedText.substring(0, 50)` — exact match.
- Line 152: outer `catch (error)` closing the try opened at line 47.
- Line 159: `res.status(500).json({ error: 'Translation failed' });` — the generic 500 that masks the real cause.

`data` is the result of `await response.json()` (line 127) from node-fetch, which is untyped/`unknown`, so the access chain `data.data.translations[0].translatedText` is entirely unvalidated at runtime. The `!response.ok` guard only covers non-2xx responses; an HTTP 200 with an unexpected body (`{}`, missing `data`, or empty `translations` array) reaches line 140 and throws a TypeError, which is swallowed by the catch at line 152 and reported as a generic 500. The finding's analysis of behavior is exactly right.

Severity assessment is fair: the catch block means the instance does not crash, and the trigger (Google Translate returning 200 with a malformed body) is uncommon, so 'low' is appropriate. The downside is real but limited to error-message quality / debuggability rather than availability or security.

The proposed fix is correct and idiomatic (optional chaining + type guard, returning 502 for an upstream bad response). Only a trivial nit: the rest of the handler uses 500 for internal errors and the upstream `response.status` for API errors (line 136); 502 is semantically defensible for the unexpected-shape case and does not undermine the fix. No correction needed.

surviving.title: LovesIngy JSON-message path writes `message: undefined` and an invalid Timestamp when JSON lacks expected fields
surviving.description: handleAddLoveMessage tries `JSON.parse(newLoveMessage)` (line 163) and, if the result is any object, overwrites messageData with `{ message: parsedMessage.text, timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp / 1000, parsedMessage.timestamp % 1000) }` (lines 165-168). There is NO validation that `parsedMessage.text` or `parsedMessage.timestamp` exist. If a user types valid JSON that happens to be an object without those keys (e.g. `{"a":1}`, or even an array since `typeof [] === 'object'`), this writes `message: undefined` to Firestore and constructs `new Timestamp(NaN, NaN)`. The Firestore SDK will reject `undefined` fields (addDoc throws) OR, if it serializes, the reader at line 64 (`message: data.message`) and the renderer `stripTrmnlPrefix(message)` (line 145, `msg.replace(...)`) will throw on undefined. The TRMNL sync (scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs:52, `doc.data().message?.startsWith('>>>')` and line 75 `.replace(...)` without optional chaining) would also break on a doc whose `message` is missing. The JSON branch was clearly meant for a specific ingest format ({text, timestamp}); it should validate that shape.
surviving.evidence: const parsedMessage = JSON.parse(newLoveMessage); if (parsedMessage && typeof parsedMessage === 'object') { messageData = { message: parsedMessage.text, timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp / 1000, parsedMessage.timestamp % 1000) }; }
surviving.recommendation: Validate the parsed shape before taking the JSON branch, otherwise fall through to the plain-string path: `if (parsedMessage && typeof parsedMessage.text === 'string' && typeof parsedMessage.timestamp === 'number') { messageData = { message: parsedMessage.text, timestamp: Timestamp.fromMillis(parsedMessage.timestamp) }; }`. (Note: prefer Timestamp.fromMillis over `new Timestamp(ms/1000, ms%1000)` — the latter is wrong anyway since the second arg is nanoseconds, not sub-millisecond remainder.) Separately, harden trmnl-sync.mjs:75 with `selectedDoc.data().message?.replace(...) ?? ''` to match the optional chaining already used at line 52. The reader at LovesIngy.tsx:64 (`data.message ?? ''`) is optional defensive hardening but is not actually reachable with a bad value through the current write path, since the SDK throws on undefined/NaN before persisting.
surviving.verifier_note:
The cited code is reproduced exactly. LovesIngy.tsx:163-168 does JSON.parse(newLoveMessage) and, for ANY object/array (typeof []==='object' is true), overwrites messageData with {message: parsedMessage.text, timestamp: new Timestamp(parsedMessage.timestamp/1000, parsedMessage.timestamp%1000)} with zero validation that .text/.timestamp exist. For {"a":1} that yields message:undefined and Timestamp(NaN,NaN). So the missing-validation bug is REAL and correctly located.

However the finding is mis-scoped on impact/severity. The key claim "this writes message: undefined to Firestore" is largely false in practice: (1) firebase.ts:32 initializes Firestore via initializeFirestore WITHOUT ignoreUndefinedProperties, so the default (false) means addDoc rejects undefined fields and throws; (2) the Firestore Timestamp constructor itself validates seconds/nanoseconds and throws on NaN/non-finite values before addDoc even runs. Either way the throw is caught by the outer try/catch at line 183. So the malformed doc is NOT persisted through this path, and the described cascade — reader at line 64 (data.message), renderer stripTrmnlPrefix at 145/373, and trmnl-sync.mjs line 75 (.replace without optional chaining) — only triggers if a doc lacking `message` is created by some OTHER means. trmnl-sync.mjs:52 already uses optional chaining (message?.startsWith); line 75 does NOT, so that unguarded .replace is a genuine latent issue but not reached via this UI path.

The actual observed defect is a UX/robustness one: typing valid JSON that isn't {text, timestamp} causes the write to silently throw, the catch at 185 only alerts on permission-denied (so the user sees nothing), and the input is never cleared. For a single-user private app with no data actually corrupted, medium overstates it; low fits better. The proposed fix (validate text:string + timestamp:number, else fall through to plain-string) is correct and is the right remedy; the suggested defensive guards on the reader and trmnl-sync .replace are reasonable hardening.

surviving.title: love-ingy-messages onSnapshot query has no limit — unbounded read growth, and orderBy implies a single-field index dependency
surviving.description: The client subscribes to the entire `love-ingy-messages` collection with `query(loveMessagesRef, orderBy('timestamp', 'desc'))` and no `limit(...)` (line 57). Every message ever written is downloaded and held in React state on every page load and live-updated forever. This is fine at small scale but grows unbounded — the TRMNL sync already caps its own read at `.limit(200)` (scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs:36), showing the collection is expected to grow. Cost and memory scale linearly with total messages. A single-field `orderBy` does not require a composite index (Firestore auto-creates single-field indexes), so no missing-index error here — but if a `where` clause is ever added alongside this orderBy it will need a composite index (none exist: there is no firestore.indexes.json in the repo). The Progress component's `onSnapshot(collection(db,'element-tracker'))` (Progress.tsx:222) is similarly unbounded but is genuinely single-user/one-doc-per-day, so lower concern.
surviving.evidence: const queryOrderByTimestamp = query(loveMessagesRef, orderBy('timestamp', 'desc'));  // no limit()
surviving.recommendation: Bound the live query, but note two things the original fix omits: (1) `limit` is not currently imported — add it to the firebase/firestore import block (lines 5-16). (2) Capping the onSnapshot also caps `loveMessages.length`, so the message counter on line 363 would max out at 200 and no longer reflect the true total. If the true count matters, fetch it separately (a getCountFromServer aggregation query) rather than deriving it from the truncated live array. Concretely: `import { ..., limit } from "firebase/firestore";` then `const queryOrderByTimestamp = query(loveMessagesRef, orderBy('timestamp','desc'), limit(200));` to match the sync cap, and if an accurate total is desired, drive line 363 from a separate count query instead of loveMessages.length.
surviving.verifier_note: Re-read src/components/LovesIngy/LovesIngy.tsx in full. Every cited fact checks out. Line 57 is exactly `const queryOrderByTimestamp = query(loveMessagesRef, orderBy('timestamp', 'desc'));` with no limit(); the firebase/firestore import block (lines 5-16) does not even import `limit`. The query feeds onSnapshot (line 59) which maps ALL snapshot docs into loveMessages state (line 68) and renders every one (lines 366-378), so cost/memory grow linearly with total messages held live forever. The sync-cap claim is verified: scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs orders the same `love-ingy-messages` collection by timestamp desc with `.limit(200)`, confirming the collection is expected to grow. No firestore.indexes.json exists in the repo (verified via ls). Progress.tsx lines 221-222 confirmed as onSnapshot(collection(db,'element-tracker')) — unbounded but genuinely one-doc-per-day single-user, correctly noted as lower concern. The Firestore index reasoning is correct: a single-field orderBy uses an auto-created single-field index (no composite index needed, no missing-index error today), and adding a where alongside it would require a composite index. Severity low is appropriate: this is a private app for a couple of users, so unbounded growth is a real scale-hygiene issue, not an active bug. The only gaps are in the proposed fix's completeness, not its correctness.

surviving.title: trmnl-config countdowns write replaces whole doc with no schema validation (lost-update + malformed event risk)
surviving.description: saveCountdowns does `setDoc(docRef, { events: updatedEvents })` (line 106) — a full document overwrite of trmnl-config/countdowns built from the in-memory `countdownEvents` array. Two of the three whitelisted users editing concurrently will silently clobber each other (last-write-wins on the whole array). Events are not schema-validated before write: `newEvent` requires only name+date (line 117), and the reader in trmnl-sync.mjs:100-108 assumes `event.date` parses (`new Date(event.date + 'T00:00:00')`) and reads `event.name`/`event.emoji`. The web reader (line 89-90) defensively checks `Array.isArray(events)`, but trmnl-sync.mjs:95 does `const events = config.events || []` then `.map` with no array guard — if `events` were ever written as a non-array object the sync `.map` throws. Rules (firestore.rules:15-22) correctly gate access to the 3 whitelisted emails, so this is an integrity/UX concern among trusted users, not an external security hole.
surviving.evidence: await setDoc(docRef, { events: updatedEvents });  // full overwrite, no validation  /// trmnl-sync.mjs: const events = config.events || []; ... events.map(event => new Date(event.date + 'T00:00:00'))
surviving.recommendation: All proposed fixes are valid; prioritize them as follows. (1) Highest value / lowest cost: in scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs add an array guard — change line 95 to `const events = Array.isArray(config.events) ? config.events : []` (and optionally filter out events missing a valid `date` before `.map` to avoid Invalid Date entries). This removes the crash asymmetry with the web reader. (2) For the lost-update/staleness issue, the most direct fix in LovesIngy.tsx is to switch the countdowns loader from one-shot `getDoc` to `onSnapshot` so the in-memory array stays current, OR wrap the read-modify-write in `runTransaction`. Splitting each countdown into its own subcollection doc (as proposed) is the most robust but a larger change for a 3-user app. (3) Schema validation before write is reasonable: enforce `name` is a non-empty trimmed string and `date` matches /^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/; the emoji-is-string check is low priority since the UI already constrains it.
surviving.verifier_note:
I re-read all three cited files and every claim holds.

LovesIngy.tsx:106 — `await setDoc(docRef, { events: updatedEvents });` is exactly that: a full-document overwrite (no `{ merge: true }`, no field-level update) of trmnl-config/countdowns, built from the in-memory `countdownEvents` state. Confirmed.

Concurrency claim confirmed and actually stronger than stated: the reader at lines 81-100 uses a one-shot `getDoc` (not `onSnapshot`), so the in-memory array is loaded once on mount and never refreshed. Both `addCountdownEvent` (122-126) and `deleteCountdownEvent` (131-134) compute `updatedEvents` from this potentially-stale state, then full-overwrite. So two of the three whitelisted users editing concurrently (or even the same user across stale sessions) will last-write-wins clobber the whole array.

Validation claim confirmed: addCountdownEvent (117) only checks `newEvent.name.trim()` and `newEvent.date`; nothing validates shape at the data layer before write. (Minor nuance: `date` originates from `<input type="date">` with a `min`, and emoji from a fixed EMOJI_OPTIONS picker defaulting to '🎉', so in normal UI flow these are well-formed — the emoji validation in the proposed fix is largely theoretical.)

Reader asymmetry confirmed and is the strongest point: web reader at line 90 guards `Array.isArray(events) ? events : []`, but trmnl-sync.mjs:95 does `const events = config.events || []` and line 99-100 immediately calls `events.map(...)` with no array guard. If `events` were ever a non-array truthy value, the sync `.map` throws TypeError. trmnl-sync.mjs:101 does `new Date(event.date + 'T00:00:00')` and reads `event.name`/`event.emoji` (105-106) — confirmed, would produce Invalid Date / undefined name on malformed events.

Rules confirmed: firestore.rules:15-22 gate trmnl-config read+write to the same 3 whitelisted emails. So this is an integrity/UX concern among trusted users plus a possible sync-job crash, not an external security hole.

Severity `low` is correct: external attack surface is closed by the rules; failure modes require either rare concurrent edits among 3 trusted users or manually/externally malformed data. The proposed fix is sound; the `Array.isArray(config.events)` guard in trmnl-sync.mjs is the highest-value, lowest-cost part.

surviving.title: firestore.rules: default-deny confirmed correct — app uses exactly the 3 declared collections
surviving.description: Verified the rules concern from the brief: firestore.rules declares match blocks ONLY for love-ingy-messages, trmnl-config, and element-tracker; with rules_version '2' and no catch-all, every other path is default-deny. A repo-wide grep for `collection(db`/`doc(db` confirms the app touches only those three collections: element-tracker (Progress.tsx:222,272,392,485 and scripts/seed-mock-data.ts:103), love-ingy-messages (LovesIngy.tsx:56,155 and trmnl-sync.mjs:33,82), and trmnl-config/countdowns (LovesIngy.tsx:84,105 and trmnl-sync.mjs:90). So no client read/write is silently blocked by missing rules, and there are no orphan collections. One nuance worth noting: element-tracker is restricted to a single email (firestore.rules:27) while the Cloud Function's ALLOWED_EMAILS (index.ts:8-12) and the love/trmnl rules whitelist 3 emails — these are intentionally different scopes, but the duplication (same email list hard-coded in firestore.rules, functions/src/index.ts, and src/config.ts) is a drift risk if one is updated without the others.
surviving.evidence: match /love-ingy-messages/{document} ... match /trmnl-config/{document} ... match /element-tracker/{document} — and grep shows no other collection(db,...) / doc(db,...) references in src/ or scripts/
surviving.recommendation: No rules change needed. Optionally de-duplicate the email whitelist — rules can't import JS, but the function (index.ts) and client (src/config.ts) could share one constant, with a comment in firestore.rules pointing to the canonical list to keep the three in sync.
surviving.verifier_note:
Re-read all cited files and they support the finding. firestore.rules (read in full) declares match blocks for exactly three collections: love-ingy-messages (lines 5-12), trmnl-config (lines 15-22), and element-tracker (lines 25-28), under rules_version '2' (line 1) with no catch-all wildcard match. So Firestore's default-deny applies to every other path — the claim is correct. (Minor nit: the description anchors this to firestore.rules:3, which is the `match /databases/{database}/documents` line; the substance is right.)

I independently re-ran the grep across src/, scripts/, AND functions/ for collection(/doc(/collectionGroup(. The complete result set is exactly: element-tracker at Progress.tsx:222,272,392,485 and seed-mock-data.ts:103; love-ingy-messages at LovesIngy.tsx:56,155 and trmnl-sync.mjs:34,82; trmnl-config('countdowns') at LovesIngy.tsx:84,105 and trmnl-sync.mjs:90. No other collections, no dynamic/variable collection names, no orphan collections. This fully corroborates the 'only three collections, no silently-blocked client access' conclusion. (One citation is off by one: the love-ingy ref in trmnl-sync.mjs is line 34, the finding said 33 — trivial, non-substantive.)

functions/src/index.ts is a Google Translate proxy that performs no Firestore reads/writes, so it correctly has no dependency on collection rules; it only enforces ALLOWED_EMAILS for the HTTP endpoint.

The duplication/drift observation is verified and real: the same 3-email whitelist is hard-coded in firestore.rules (lines 8-10 and 18-20), functions/src/index.ts (lines 8-12), and src/config.ts (lines 1-5), while element-tracker is intentionally restricted to a single email (firestore.rules:27). These are genuinely three separate copies that can drift.

Severity info is appropriate — this is a verification/no-bug finding plus a low-stakes maintainability note. The proposed fix is sound: no rules change needed, optionally share one JS constant between index.ts and config.ts with a comment in firestore.rules. I'd only add that index.ts and config.ts live in separate build roots (functions/ vs src/) so they can't trivially import one shared module without build tooling; a single source-of-truth would likely need a generated file or a code comment cross-reference rather than a literal shared import. That refinement does not change the verdict.

surviving.title: functions/package.json deps lag installed majors (firebase-admin ^12 declared, modular API now used elsewhere)
surviving.description: functions/package.json declares firebase-admin ^12.0.0 (line 16) and firebase-functions ^7.0.2 (line 17), engines.node '22' (line 12), with a committed functions/package-lock.json (the deploy workflow installs functions via npm, per the known half-migrated deploy.yml). Meanwhile scripts/trmnl-sync.mjs was deliberately migrated to the firebase-admin modular API and the workflow pins admin @^14 (per the file's own header comment and the already-known TRMNL fix). The Cloud Function itself uses `admin.initializeApp()` + `admin.auth()` (index.ts:6,57) which is the namespaced API still present in v12 — so this builds fine today. The risk is the inconsistency: the function pins admin v12 while the sync job runs v14, and an unpinned bump of the function's admin to v14 would hit the same default-export removal that broke the sync (the function only uses `admin.auth()`/`initializeApp`, both still available via `firebase-admin/auth`, so the eventual migration is low-effort). Node 22 engine matches Gen2 defaults — fine.
surviving.evidence: "firebase-admin": "^12.0.0", "firebase-functions": "^7.0.2" (functions/package.json) vs trmnl-sync.mjs modular imports + workflow pin @^14

Not urgent — the function is correct as-is.
